dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 59

Transcribed from container: "Diana Ross." 

The interview begins around 3 minutes. On this tape, there is lots of commotion and sound like many people are trying to interview Diana Ross on set as she prepares for her next show. Ross talks about a book she's writing called Help Help; juggling family and career; how she feels leaving The Supremes; her acting experience; and other inspirational artists in the show business. At 28:30, the tape appears to transition to another interview. Interviewers who were waiting nearby to ask her questions ask what her greatest fear is and asked the name of her show. Finally at 35:26, the SOUL Magazine interviewer gets a clear recording with Diana and asks if she will be doing a movie called Careshadora, based on Coriea Jones’ novel.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

On side A, Bill Cosby discusses Mother, Jugs & Speed film; growing up Black in Mississippi; how he goes about selecting a script; family; his personal morals around the holidays, Christmas and Kwanza; how he reacts to the way people treat him in the professional world, such as his feeling around his sometimes lack of recognition in the Black community; the Three Levels of Human Laughter; the misuses of the "n" word; and future projects in film. 

On side B, Cosby discusses Richard Pryor. Also on Side B, from around 3:40, a Muhammad Ali recording begins. At a press conference after a fight against George Foreman, Ali discusses his next fight against Joe Frazier and Ali's plans for retirement.
Collection PASC 342, Box 58

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "James Earl Jones." 

On side A, James Earl Jones talks about growing up in Mississippi; being raised by his grandmother, who was a Black militant; growing up in the Methodist church and his own "spiritual death"; his first acting role on Broadway; what he believes the keys to success are; the canonization of American leaders, like Malcolm X, Martin Luther King, Jr., Booker T. Washington, and others; his roles in films The River Niger, The UFO Incident, and The Bingo Long Traveling All- Stars & Motor Kings; and his personal hobbies in addition to his world stardom. 

On side B, Jones answers, "who is the real James Earl Jones?"; his Oscar nomination; and what he'd do if he could retire tomorrow.
Collection PASC 342, Box 60

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Mary Wilson/Pedro Ferrer/Marco Turk (lawyer) by L.P.J. 1977 December 8." 

L.P.J. is Leonard J. Pitts. Mary Wilson talks about growing up in Detroit; how she feels after receiving the Golden Record; being in the second best selling group next to The Beatles; and what years with the Supremes have taught her. Marco Turc and Pedro Ferrer--involved with negotiating the legal matters with Motown--also provide insight on the legal details of Mary's leaving the Supremes, as they continue to record as a group.

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 61

Transcribed from container: "The Emotions and Teddy Pendergrass by Tsuyoko Sako, January 6 1976; Lonne Elder III, undated." 

On side A, the Emotions -- Wanda Hutchinson, Jennette Hutchinson-Hauss, Sheila Hutchinson-Witt -- talk about marriage, family, and how involved they are in the business management side of their careers. They also talk about Jeanette dropping out of the group, working with Stax Records, and how they feel about the release of their first album. 

On side B, Teddy Pendergrass speaks about the misprints in articles that claimed he was new to the group, the Blue Notes, while creating the To Be True album; issues working with Harold; his plans to change his sound; and what is on his agenda for his new group, the Blue Notes. He also talks about the group's album, Wake Up, going gold and potentially platinum. He clarifies rumors about Larry Brown and his lack of appearance at their Apollo performance. The tape cuts out at 10:05 and returns at 13:32 to a man named Lonny Elder speaking about how film studios should reach out to new and fresh Black creatives and writers.

Transcribed from container: "Bill Withers by Regina Jones #2 and #3, Los Angeles, October 16 1975." 

Bill Withers talks about differentiating between the expectations of labels of "entertaining" within the industry and oneself. He also discusses family and philosophizes on getting to know himself. He speaks about the opportunities he's had; what he created while he couldn't legally make music; and what it's like to be an entrepreneur in the business.

Transcribed from container: "Bill Withers by Regina Jones #1, Los Angeles, October 16 1975." 

On side A, Bill Withers talks about his new album, which came out almost 2 years after his previous release; his creative process; his roots in West Virgina; his lawsuit with Sussec Record Company; living in the limelight versus having a private life; and setting up his new production company. 

On side B, Withers talks about how to better oneself; a statement he made in Jet Magazine; how he measures his value; and what he seeks in life and what makes him happy.

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Diane Steinberg by Judy Spiegelman, February 24 1978." 

On side A, Diane Steinberg talks about growing up in Detroit in a Black and Jewish family; her album, Universal Child; her relationship with religion and spirituality; her age; description of the film she is in; the challenges of juggling life and acting. 

On side B, Steinberg talks about the pressures of being onscreen; taking her shot in the pop genre; white people being able to decide what "Black music" is; plans for her career with RSO; changing agencies; getting her album back from ABC; and how writing works as her therapy.
Collection PASC 342, Box 61

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 60

Transcribed from container: "Cicely Tyson 1976 March 3." 

On side A, Cicely Tyson talks about her strict upbringing in the church, her spiritual journey, and discovering what God means to her. She discusses what she enjoys about acting; her insight on peoples' attraction to theater and film; imagination versus concentration; and what she learned from starring as Jane Pittman in The Autobiography of Miss Jane Pittman. 

On side B, Tyson discusses her process of decompressing or detoxing after playing a role; The River Niger storyline; how she feels about receiving an Oscar; and if she would consider writing her own scripts.

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 57

Transcribed from container: "Belafonte and Tina Turner, ca. 1972." 

On side A, Henry Belafonte talks about his zodiac sign; the movie, The Angel Lavine; his opinion on current Black films being made; the development of the film industry in East Africa; the last time he was on television and myths of Blacks on TV; the obliteration of these myths by films, such as Carmen Jones and Island in the Sun; and fighting for liberation, not only in Hollywood but globally. He also speaks on the Civil Rights Movement; the start of his production company;  singing; preaching; his wife and children; and the ambivalence of the white community.  

On side B, Belafonte continues talking about white reviewers of amateur Black films and the implications of problematic narratives; the representation of history; and the global struggle for freedom by marginalized people. He also discusses working with the SNCC and his wife's involvement in Black spaces and liberation movements. At 16:39, the recording switches to an interview with Tina Turner, who discusses the kinds of people she enjoys working with; her experiences as Black woman in the entertainment industry; her role in Ike Turner's band; gender roles in relationships; and what she believes makes a woman.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 57

Transcribed from container: "Gladys Knight & the Pips undated." 

On side A, Gladys Knight discusses her experiences as a women on tour and in the industry and her origin story. Gladys and the Pips discuss growing up together; how the group came to be, being discovered by Motown; maintaining excellence in the public eye; what a typical week is like; their opinions on songs that tell stories; whether record companies have the same responsibilities as the artist in conveying messages or maintaining certain images; and appealing to younger audiences. 

On side B, they talk about marketability of lyrics and songs; appealng to different audiences; conflicts in the politics of Black musicians gaining notoriety in magazines; and being uplifted by Black labels now versus the historical lack of a Black support network in the industry before Motown, Jet, and SOUL. They also discuss the politics of Black hair, style, and owning oneself despite criticism or lack of representation.Undated recording most likely made between 1967 and 1989.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

On side A, Ken Jones interviews American and Carribean actors and participants of the first annual Jamaican Film Festival. Sam Greenely, Calvin Lockhart, Paula Kelly, Brock Peters, Bernie Casey, Grace McGee, Cicely Tyson, Stan Lathan, Richard Murcia, and Bernie Masey win awards. On side B, Ken Jones interviews David Ruffen about his success since leaving the Temptations and songs on his new album. At 4:15, the recording goes back to Cicely Tyson's interview, which is the same as the recording on side B of uclapasc0342_060_008.

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 57

Transcribed from container: "B.B. King, January 1970." 

On side A, the recording begins with a phone call. The interview with B.B. King begins at 3:30. King talks about growing up in Mississippi; how he got into music; working on a farm to avoid being shipped to the war; moving to Memphis; and starting his gigs on the road. 

On side B, King talks about getting gigs; becoming well recognized in Memphis; and seeing the rise of artists, like Roosevelt Sikes, Joe Liggens, Charles Brown, and T-Bone Walker. He also discusses his experience recording Martha King and Take a Swing with Me; signing his first contract with Modern Record Company in 1949; the evolution of music techinology; and the instruments primarily uses on tracks. He share his opinion on the discovery of Black music; how people have paved the way for white artists to steal from those artists; what the Blues is; when he thinks he'll have national success; Ike and Tina Turner; and his rise to being accepted as an artist. At 19:08, the interview cuts off, then returns.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 57

Transcribed from container: "Stevie Wonder ca. 1960s." 

On side A, Stevie Wonder talks about how he started at Motown; what he was doing before and his experience as a child star; his experience with blindness; how he develops impressions of people. He also talks about his reputation as a 19 year old; his girlfriend and being an open relationship; and how Barry Gordy gave him his stage name. Wonder leaves to answer a phone call at 19:32, and returns to the interview at 26:10; he also leaves to answer calls at 30:00 and 53:25.

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Susaye Green, December 7, 1977" 

On side A, Susaye Greene talks about what it was like growing up; where she was before Wonderlove; her background working with the Supremes; the growing popularity of women's singing groups; and what it's like working with Stevie Wonder with the Raylettes. 

On side B, Susaye Greene discusses the new group of the Supremes and how they'll succeed.

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Bobby Womack, John Amos, The Brothers Johnson, February 23 1976." 

On side A, Bobby Womack speaks about his efforts to support Jack Wise and his family, while Wise is hospitalized; controversy with James Brown; his plans for Salute Foundation, which supports artists that may not have received recognition they deserved or were cheated out of money and success in the industry. He speaks about an event at the Palladium, which include artists such as, Wilson Pickett, Al Green, Aretha Franklin, HB Bottoms, Stevie Wonder, Richard Pryor, Barry White, Rod Stewart, James Taylor, Carly Simon, Mick Jagger, and Redd Foxx. Lastly he discusses his intentions to make the event palatable for all audiences, not just a "black" event.  

On side B, John Amos speaks about the highlight of the SOUL album and its being highlighted in local radio stations. Amos discusses the quote that will be used for a consumer ad; Credo Mutwa, the South African witch doctor; and Richard Rainbow.
Collection PASC 342, Box 62
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 62

Transcribed from container: "Natalie Cole, 1975." 

On side A, Natalie Cole talks about how many interviews she's been on in since she's been on tour; doing drugs but eventually finding God through music; the rock 'n roll theme of her music; her sister and her acting career; her record company Capital;  her songs, "Your Face," and "Inseparable." She also talks about how she is perceived in the world and whether this image is an accurate representation of her; how long she's been back on the east coast; and her experience coming into blackness during her college experiences at USC and University of Massachusetts, at a time during the large protests on college campuses and Black social movements were gaining momentum. 

On side B, she continues talking about her experiences on the west coast, specifically in California, in comparison to the east coast. She also compares men on both coasts, which on radio stations are playing her record, her management fund, and her upcoming performance with Freddy Hovery.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 62

Transcribed from container: "Muhammad Ali, January 4 1979." 

On side A, Muhammad Ali talks about the role he played in a historical film; his relationship with his God, Allah; what he feels his responsibilities are at his level of success; youth that look up to him; and his international presence as Muhammad Ali. He also discusses upcoming and previous fights with Sphinx and Frazier; how he perceives the Nation of Islam; and whether he will let boxing rest. He talks about his world organization; his appeal to world leaders, like Farrakhan in South Africa; and whether he perceives himself as a leader. Lastly, on the tape, there is a recording of church choir rehearsal. 

On side B, there is a recording of an opera. About 11 minutes in, the tape switches to recording of an interview of a man reading a poem about the heart. After 19 minutes, the tape switches back to the opera recording.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: " Smokey Robinson and Isaac Hayes(?) undated." 

Side A is the same recording as recording from Smokey Robinson at the Century Plaza uclapasc0342_057_001 (30 min). 

On side B, the interviewer asks interviewee--possibly Isaac Hayes based on the information transcribed from the container--about his record I Was Born this Way, describing it as a gay liberation record.  The interviewer proceeds with a list of questions inquiring about the LGBTQ community to which Hayes (?) describes his personal experience with his sexuality. The interviewer also references an article by Bunny Jones in Entertainment magazine; Haye's educational and musical background; his active role in the liberation movement; how he feels about his first record being judged on its "controversial merits"; and his thoughts on Richard Pryor's recent comments on the gay community.
Collection PASC 342, Box 59

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Maurice White 1977 October 26."  

On side A, Maurice White talks about the need for positive influences on Black kids; growing up in a Black family in Memphis; his intentions moving forward in his career and supporting others along the way; his collaboration with the group, the Emotions; and the differences between him and his siblings. 

On side B, White talks about God's will leading him in his production creativity; other current artists creating "higher frequency music"; bringing purpose and positivity to the world; and how to appeal to different and global audiences.
Collection PASC 342, Box 58

title_tesim: Interviews with Jesse Jackson, George Reeves, and Ronnie by Mike Terrence
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
services_contact_ssm: Property rights to the physical objects belong to UCLA Library Special Collections. All other rights, including copyright, are retained by the creators and their heirs. dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 62

Transcribed from container: "Jesse Jackson, George Reeves, and Ronnie by Mike Terry, September 1978; George Kerr; Ronnie Dyson, New York, 1970." 

On side A, Jesse Jackson talks about the resolution of the Redd Foxx situation and his role in mediation; the president of ABC, George Reeves; Jackson's plans to meet with Mick Jagger and his objections to Jagger's obscene lyrics in relation to Black girls. After this interview concludes, Mike Terrence interviews George Reeves about the Redd Foxx situation. After this call, Terrence switches to a call with Jesse Jackson. Jackson discusses his original venture towards professional football; his goals for Black artists; and his typical working environment in the studio and with artists, like Linda Jones. 

On side B, a 20-year-old singer named Ronnie speaks about coming into his success on Broadway; his biggest inspiration, Nat King Cole; whether he took professional classes; his typical opening and closing acts/songs; his opinion on the popularity and negative effects of narcotics amongst artists; growing up singing in the church; his style, dedication, and attitude towards performing; and his first album release. Ronnie's mother speaks about her past experience singing in the church and what it's like having a son earn success at such a young age.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Richard Pryor press conference and Danielle Spencer undated." 

On side A, at the beginning of the tape, Danielle Spencer talks about American Bandstand and Soul Train; artists to whom she enjoys listening; and her general dislike of concerts. The interviewer also mentions working on a cover issue about women in the industry. Beginning at 6:59, on side A, is a press conference with Richard Pryor. Pryor discusses the issue with censorship on television and limits to his creative freedom. At 9:00, a clip is shown of Pryor's advertisement for his new show. Pryor then discusses the issue the network had with this clip, and he continues to answer the press's questions about other reasons why the network may have censored his clip. 

On side B, a cast member of What's Happening! discusses what it's like to be 11 or 12 and working, how they like acting, and keeping up with childhood and school while working.
Collection PASC 342, Box 60
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 61

Transcribed from container: "Stevie Wonder by Lee Underwood, November 1975." 

On side A, Stevie Wonder talks about his next album releasing, which will be a double album; goes over the concept of Ordinary Pain on his new album; whether he was pressured to release the album for the Grammys; how he feels listening to his old songs; staying loyal to himself; what his plans are for his new $13 million, 7-year contract with Motown. He also talks about how, as a multimillionaire, he knows who to trust; the constant comparisons between him and Elton John; being a father; and why he hasn't transitioned to television. 

On side B, Wonder talks about his goal to have a national TV show and his own record; his open relationship with his wife; whether he considers himself a sex symbol on stage; his appendicitis; and his plans to transcribe the new album.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 57

Transcribed from container: "Smokey Robinson at the Century Plaza"

It is unclear who the speaker is on side A. They talk about growing up queer and being questioned about their sexuality. They also talk about being a a drum major and performing music. At 2:28, the initial interview cuts off and goes into an interview with Isaac Hayes. Hayes discusses a Sam Marcos cover of AIP; getting poor financial advice; being advised to sue Stax Records; having financial issues with his new contract with ABC; Jim Magazine framing him as a spendthrifty amidst his financial hardship; being investigated by the IRS; the Black Moses image; whether he feels his music is "Black music"; and how he feels about the new label handling his work. 

On side B, Calvin Lockhart discusses being Bahamian, his kinship to Black American people, the film Joanna, other films he's acted in, and the profits made from the film Shaft.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 62

Transcribed from container: "Mick Jagger by Regina Jones, August 16 1978." 

On this tape, Mick Jagger talks about the headline of SOUL's article on him titled, "White Artist Selling Negro Soul," which was in the first issue of SOUL. Jones asks his intentions for specifically promoting to Black audiences and his interest in the Black press; and if he has any feelings about young white artists playing the blues and other old black genres. He talks about playing with Bob Marley and being influenced by Stevie Wonder; whether he believes in God; finding complete satisfaction in life; his love for Reggae music; if he's ever felt any resentment from Black performers he's admired, due to the success he's had; his response to the backlash of his comment about Black ladies liking to "ball all night"; his lawsuit with Marsha Hunt; children; Peter Tarsh; his feelings around gaining success; and his plans for touring.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Mr. B (i.e. Mark Lincoln Braun) #3 and Sammy D. (i.e. Sammy Davis, Jr.), August 1979." 

On side A, Mark Lincoln Braun talks about people who have have been influential in his career; producing Sarah Vaughn; what his goals are in this stage of his career; artists he feels have the potential for longevity; his kids; his future goals; his proudest career moment; and what direction he sees music going. 

On side B, there is static, and the tape begins at 8:50. Sammy Davis, Jr. talks about his major influences, such as Billy Eckstein, Frank Sinatra, Al McCibben, and the Nicholar Brothers; and his favorite Eckstein story.

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Donna Summer and Rufus Band member, September 29, 1978." 

On side A, Donna Summers talks about her sisters involvement as a backup singer on her songs; balancing family and her career; her pride in the title of "Disco Queen"; the genre of disco; her growth since her hit single Love to Love You Baby; her image as a sex symbol; plans for the future. 

On side B, Donna talks about her experience directing; responds to questions for the SOUL Christmas issue; and the Diana Ross Vegas show. Also on side B, around 13:25, there is an interview with the member of the Rufus band, who discusses Chaka Khan's separation from the group.
Collection PASC 342, Box 58

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Mabel King "What's Happening" by Walter Burrell 1978 July 18." 

Walter Burrell, journalist from SOUL talks with a female speaker about Mabel King's reappearance on What's Happening! After confirming that she will not be returning, this speaker then clarifies what will happen in King's stead. At 2:13, Burrell speaks to Mr. Hornstein about whether Mabel will be returning on the next season, to which Hornstein does not fully deny or confirm. At 4:20, Burrell asks Mabel King, who clarifies that she was supposed to be negotiating the terms of her contract. She also addresses some of her issues with the portrayal of Black characters in the script and the unfairness of pay in the industry.
Collection PASC 342, Box 59

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Redd Foxx quits and Aretha Franklin/Glynn Turman." 

On side A, a person records a letter to be sent to Bob Jones regarding interview protocol. At 1:55, it switches to a recording of a CBS live-broadcasting about Farrah Fawcet filing a lawsuit against Redd Fox. In an interview, Redd expresses that he will not be apologizing and is done working with the ABC network, and will not be doing any more shows. The tape cuts off between 7:55 and 8:32. The end of side A is muffled and distant, but sounds like a continued discussion of Foxx's decision to leave the network. 

On side B, the tape begins at 8:15. Regina Jones interviews Aretha Franklin and asks about her trip to Europe; dating Glynn Turman; acting; the release of her album, The Almighty Fire: Woman of the Future; and plans for the rest of the year upon returning from Europe.
Collection PASC 342, Box 60

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Lou Rawls at NBC and Lana Rawls interviews by Ken Jones, April 7 & April 14, 1969." 

On side A, Lana Rawls talks about her relationship with husband, Lou Rawls; their home and luxury lifestyle; family; Lou's creativity in clothing design; their experience with a recent buglary;  Lou as a family man; and potential career plans for Lou Jr. At 23:28, the recording swithes to an interview with Lou Rawls. Ken Jones asks about a Western film, Black history, and false narratives of Black history. Lou Rawls discusses his acting career and continuing music. 

On side B, Lana Rawls talks about her upbringing, family, competing in pageants and beauty shows, and her experience modeling. She speaks about her experience meeting Lou Rawls and the trials and tribulations of their dating life.
Collection PASC 342, Box 57

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 57

Transcribed from container: "Lana Rawls, April 14, 1969." 

On side A, Lana Rawls shares details of her and Lou Rawls getting married in Detroit and her father's rejection of her decision to get married to Lou. She discusses meeting up and living with Lou in Cleveland, Ohio; being an interracial couple; and her interactions with Black women that would insult or ignore her around Lou. 

On side B, Rawls compares her experiences with racism internally within her own community and externally when faced with those in the Black community. She discusses three major reports of quarreling within her marriage to Lou, which the media has reported on. She also comments on her opinion of the trend of Black men becoming successful and marrying white women; her recreational activities outside of mothering; and her weight post-labor and her appendix bursting.
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 61

Transcribed from container: "The Supremes and Ken Jones." 

On side A, there is testing in the first 40 seconds. Mary Wilson talks about where she feels the Supremes are at in their career; whether she feels conflict between family and career; and where she feels the Supremes are musically. In the next interview, Susaye Greene discusses what she sees as her role in the Supremes; the new album; featuring on Soul Train; and being single while dedicating herself to her career. Scherrie Payne then talks about her recent interview with Walter Berrell; how she feels about the new album; her feelings about the changes in the group; and if she believes the Supremes can get back to the #1 spot after losing Diana Ross. Next Ken Jones speaks on the Black Coalition; the LA Centinnel; Chuck Young; conflicting stories about Jones' contract; and the struggles of finding and retaining Black people within the news industry. 

On side B, a Stevie Wonder press conference commences, where there are discussions about his $13 million contract deal with Motown, working with Barry Gordy, and his new album coming out. The remainder of the side B recording is Wonder performing live.

Transcribed from container: "Gladys Knight & the Pips, April 1980." 

On side A Gladys Knight and the Pips talks about changing representation by Casablanca. The Pips are now "Gladys Knight and the Pips" under CBS. They discuss business decisions made my lawyers to benefit the togetherness of the groups, album success and sales of the groups, 
On side B, Knight talks about going to The Flamingo and seeing groups, such as Glen Smith and the Fables (a Canadian group); producing a project with Colombia Records; producing Glen; and what she thinks of Sundre Sing. Regina, Gladys, and The pips discuss Hero expectations of artists and the assumptions of fans, maintaining one's integrity, Gladys's album Imagination, Glady's idea to gather staple artists to put on a gospel program, because "that't where we came from", the conclude discuss where they are living and the base they were able to produce in Vegas.

title_tesim: Interviews with George Clinton, Gene McDaniels (?), and Barry White
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Department of Special Collections
services_contact_ssm: Property rights to the physical objects belong to UCLA Library Special Collections. All other rights, including copyright, are retained by the creators and their heirs. It is the responsibility of the researcher to determine who holds the copyright and pursue the copyright owner or his or her heir for permission to publish where The UC Regents do not hold the copyright. dlcs_collection_name_ssm: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002 (Collection 342). Performing Arts Special Collections, University of California, Los Angeles.
description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "George Clinton, Gene McDaniels, and Barry White undated." 

On side A, George Clinton talks about masculine and feminine energies; working with Gladys Knight; the mark of a good song writer; how musicians and producers work in a time crunch; getting into film and producing; intelligent commercialism; his favorite artists; working on the track Testify. 

At 34:13, another interview starts with George Clinton. He talks about his music now compared to 10 years ago; whether his music is influenced by political events; drug use and creativity; what he would do if he weren't in music; his use of profanity and the purpose of language in music; his fans, who he refers to as "maggots"; and his feelings on the Black press. There is mention of Elman Whitfield, Chaka Khan, Jessica Cleave, Slime, Jimi Hendrix, and the Rolling Stones. 

On side B, there are two interviews that were simultaneously recorded. In the left channel, the interviewer discusses with the interviewee--possibly Gene McDaniels based on the information transcribed from the container--how much further the band can go in their music; how he started off as a child prodigy; and how much money he makes in a year. The left channel interview ends at 5:00 minutes. In the right channel, Barry White is interviewed about returning to the studio; what to expect next on the new album; the likelihood that he'll start a new record; his critiques on the treatment of Black artists in the industry; his dissolved partnership with Larry Newel; his goals for 1976; involvement with the controversial film Coon Skin; and his investment and retirement plans.
Collection PASC 342, Box 59
member_of_collections_ssim: SOUL Publications, Inc. records, 1955-2002

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Natalie Cole and D.J. Rogers, ca. 1978." 

D.J. Rogers is likely DeWayne Julius Rogers, keyboardist. 

On side A, Natalie Cole talks about a recent vacation to France; whether people have an issue with her not performing the "role of a minister's wife"; whether people take her seriously when she speaks about religion; if she feels like men in the industry feel intimidated by her; the "infamous" Rolling Stones piece on her; her relationship with Aretha Franklin and her feelings about newer artists getting "Natalie Cole" and "Aretha Franklin" comparisons; her background "growing up white"; growing out of her father Nat King Cole's shadow and maintaining his legacy; her next album; relationships with the press; and a fond memory of Christmas for SOUL Magazine's Christmas issue. 

On side B, DJ Rogers talks about Natalie Cole's performance in Long Beach; "message music"; how long he's been singing; the gospel that inspires his music; how he reaches people off stage; his challenges in life while pursuing music; his childhood musical favorites; his wishes to collaborate with artists, such as the Isley Brothers, the Commodores, and Maurice White; and his response to a Christmas issue survey.
Collection PASC 342, Box 58

description_tesim: Collection PASC 342, Box 61

Transcribed from container: Mr Steinberg, LPJ, and Al Green. 

On side A, Judy Spiegelman, managing editor of SOUL magazine, speaks to Mr. Steinberg verifying the rumor that he is leaving Mercury Records. They discuss a new "Men Behind the Music" issue of SOUL. This interview with Mr. Steinberg ends at 1:34. At 2:00, an interview with Leonard Pitts begins, where there is a discussion about how he remains objective in his writing. The interview with Pitts ends at 6:44. The remainder of side A is an interview with Al Green. He speaks about movie critics; Jet magazine; religious emblems; working with Universal music and Memphis Sound; crossover gospel artists; his opinion on Reverend Ike's take on gospel music; his children; whether he'll be touring in the near future; and whether he believes that people that come to his church are attracted more to the brand of Al Green or to God. 

On side B, Green shares his feelings about universal music; being compared to Sam Cooke and Otis Redding; family; being out in public and being noticed; his planned trip to Africa; and whether he wishes to offer financial help as a reverend to those suffering against apartheid in South Africa.

description_tesim:
Transcribed from container: "Aretha Franklin 1977 February 5." 

On side A, Regina Jones interviews Aretha Franklin. She discusses what motivates her to write a song; the Amazing Grace album; acting in films and what appeals her to acting; what motivates her in her career; how she feels when people say that an upcoming artist is the new Chaka Khan, Natalie Cole, or Aretha Franklin; how she feels about the new gospel trends of Reverend Jesse Jackson; her favorite artists to listen to; who she trusts to critique her music; family and living in Encino; her reaction to the poem Nikki Giovanni wrote about her; her weight loss; a recent interview with Essence magazine; and watching Roots as a historical moment. 

On side B, Franklin talks about if she would ever write a music score for a movie; what she does in her down time; her favorite songs that she's written; her family and marriage with Norman; the time it took to produce Sparkle; and what her biggest challenge is at this stage in her career.

named_subject_tesim: Pitson, James, b. ca. 1658, associated name
Harmsworth, R. Leicester (Robert Leicester), Sir, 1870-1937, former owner
repository_tesim: William Andrews Clark Memorial Library
services_contact_ssm: William Andrews Clark Memorial Library, UCLA, Phone: (310) 794-5155,  Email: clark@humnet.ucla.edu
description_tesim: Provenance: On front paste-down endpaper: book stencil of lion with the name Hill; partially obscured by two different booksellers’ descriptions pasted down. On front free endpaper: "Julia Palmer, 1671" and in the same hand "I Leave this Book to Mr Joseph Bisco senior. if he out Live me otherwiss. I Leave itt to Mr James pitson apothicary" and (in a later hand) "Phillipps ms 2597."  On front paste-down endpaper: book stencil of lion with the name Hill; partially obscured by two different booksellers' descriptions pasted down.
Binding: Bound in original full dark brown calf; rebacked in 1999 with original spine laid down.

Manuscript volume of poetry by Julia Palmer, written between 1671-1673. This volume of devotional poetry clearly shows Palmer to have been a nonconformist. The poems are arranged in two "centuries" of 100 poems each (though "the first century" is missing one poem).
member_of_collections_ssim: Early Modern English Manuscripts from UCLA’s Clark Library

title_tesim: Porta veritatis, sive Compendiaria ad beatitudinem
repository_tesim: William Andrews Clark Memorial Library
services_contact_ssm: William Andrews Clark Memorial Library, UCLA, Phone: (310) 794-5155,  Email: clark@humnet.ucla.edu
description_tesim: Has been ascribed to Menasseh ben Israel (1604-57), who visited London in the mid-1650s and sold a manuscript of this work to Ralph Cudworth, the Regius Professor of Hebrew at Cambridge. Cudworth passed this manuscript to his friend Richard Kidder, the Bishop of Bath and Wells. Kidder used the Porta veritatis as a source of Jewish objections to Christianity which he could refute in his Demonstration of the Messias (London, 1699). Kidder states in the preface to Part II of his work that he has reason to think Manasseh himself was the author of Porta veritatis. An article by J.M. Hillesum in Het Boek (1928) refutes the Manassah ben Israel attribution and suggests that the author was Rabbi Pinheiro [Duarte Pinheiro/Pinhel/Pinelli], an Italian marrano. Hillesum’s attribution is supported by Cecil Roth in his book, A Life of Menasseh ben Israel (1934). Richard Kidder willed his copy of the manuscript to Balliol College, where it still is (Ms. 251). David S. Katz, in his book Philo-Semitism and the Readmission of the Jews to England, 1603-1655, notes that in addition to the Balliol manuscript there is a copy of the Porta veritatis in the Harleian manuscripts at the British Library (MS. Harl. 3427-8).This copy at the Clark Library is bound in calf, with gold tooled spine and edges. Contains ownership signatures of Samuel Heywood and Harry Hall Squire. Also contains a lengthy note, on the verso of the front flyleaf, in the hand of Samuel Heywood about the manuscript.
member_of_collections_ssim: Early Modern English Manuscripts from UCLA’s Clark Library

title_tesim: Book of songs, music and poems
repository_tesim: William Andrews Clark Memorial Library
services_contact_ssm: William Andrews Clark Memorial Library, UCLA, Phone: (310) 794-5155,  Email: clark@humnet.ucla.edu
description_tesim: Seventeenth century music and commonplace manuscript by Scottish assistant schoolmaster, musician and session clerk Robert Taitt. The volume contains Scottish and English catches and airs, with their associated lyrics, collected and compiled by Taitt. The volume also contains some writings on music theory and teaching as well as passages from Samuel Butler’s Hudibras and William Geddes’ Saints recreation, in addition to other miscellaneous poetry in Latin and English.

title_tesim: Medici antiqui omnes qui latinis literis diversorum morborum genera & remedia persecuti sunt, undique conquisiti, & uno volumine comprehensi, ut eorum, qui se medicinae studio dediderunt, commodo consulatur ...
description_tesim: Aurelii Cornelii Celsi artium liber sextus, idem medicinae liber primus -- Quinti Sereni medicina -- Trotulae curandarum aegritudinum muliebrium, ante, in, et post partum liber unicus .... -- Marcelli de medicamentis -- Scribonii Largi, medici vetustissimi de compositione medicamentorum liber -- Sorani Ephesii isagoges in artem medendi -- C. Plinii Secundi in libros de re medica -- L. Apuleii Madaurensis, philosophi platonici de herbarum virtutibus historia ... -- Libellus utilissimus de betonica quem Antonio Musae quidam, nonnulli Apuleio adscribendum autumant -- Aemilius Macer de herbarum virtutibus, cum Ioannis Atrociani commentariis longe utilissimis -- Strabi Galli hortulus -- Caelii Aureliani Siccensis in quinque libros -- Theodori Prisciani rerum medicarum lib. quat.

Venetiis : [Apud Aldi filios], MDXLVII [1547].Printer from colophon, which reads: "Apud Aldi filios, Venetiis, MDXLVII." Signatures: *-**⁶ a⁸ ²*² b-h⁸ i⁶ k¹⁰ l-z⁸ A-Q⁸ R⁶. Woodcuts: Aldine anchor-and-dolphin device on t.p. and verso of final leaf; historiated initials. Text printed in small roman type; spaces left for capitals, with guide letters. Includes index: "Herbarum et remediorum, de quibus in hisce libris medicis tractatur, plenissimus index."

Older stiff vellum binding. Extensively annotated by two previous owners: Nicolas de Nancel, in preparation for a new edition (never published); and Michel de la Vigne, physician to Louis XIII, whose ownership inscription, dated 1615, appears on t.p. De la Vigne's copious annotations in the work expand and comment on those of Nancel. For a detailed investigation of the reading, writing, and editorial practice of Nancel, including background on Nancel's annotations in UCLA's copy of Medici antiqui omnes, see Sharratt. See also a 3-page summary of his research on Nancel by history of science bookdealer Jeremy Norman available in Special Collections. Bookplated in honor of Lee Walcott, in recognition of his support of the Ahmanson-Murphy Aldine Collection. In modern beige cloth-covered clamshell box, with brown leather spine; gold-stamped box title "Medici antiqui omnes. In honor of Lee Walcott...." Purchase, Jeremy Norman, 2006.
member_of_collections_ssim: Ahmanson-Murphy Aldine and Early Italian Collection

title_tesim: Dinner given in honor of Marshall Shepherd, Los Angeles, March 1947
summary_tesim: John L. Branham was the pastor at St. Paul Baptist church.
W. D. Carter was a minister at the Friendship Baptist Church in Pasadena.
Marshall Shepherd was the U.S. Recorder of Deeds in Washington, D. C. and pastor of Philadelphia's Mount Olivet Baptist church.
Jonathan L. Caston was pastor at Trinity Baptist Church; he ran to represent District 7 on the Los Angeles City Council (he narrowly lost to Don Allen).
Leon H. Washington, Jr. was the publisher of the Los Angeles Sentinel.

Loren Miller was an attorney at the time; he later became a California Superior Court Justice in Los Angeles County. Miller was known for his work fighting for equal access to housing and education. He was also the editor of the California Eagle after Charlotta Bass.

Caston last week...Marshall expressed his best wishes for success in the current campaign to elect Rev. Caston to the city council from District 7. (Los Angeles Sentinel, 6 Mar. 1947: 5)

title_tesim: Betty Clark and Ruby Joyce, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Betty Clark (née Betty Whitfield Wilson), was the wife of Horace Clark . Mrs. Clark, a former fashion designer, had been married to a police officer Arthur Wilson. She divorced Wilson, and later married Horace P. Clark, Sr., proprietor of the Clark Hotel. After his death, she became the full-time manager of the hotel. Betty was also the sister of Mal (Malvin) Whitfield, Olympic track medalist and member of the Tuskegee Airmen who also spent 47 years in Africa training athletes.
Ruby Joyce was a well-known model in Los Angeles.

Katie Dunn was accused of killing actor Henry Washington by stabbing him in the heart. A plea of self defense was accepted by the court. She was represented by attorney Walter L. Gordon, Jr. and the case was heard by Judge Samuel Gates.

Ivan J. Johnson III was a Los Angeles attorney. He was one of the first African Americans to letter in track at USC and UC Berkeley. He was a charter member of the Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity. Shortly before his death, he was honored by the Langston Law Club as the dean of  local attorneys.
Ethel Sissle (1915-1984) was maried to the band leader Noble Sissle, and later to Walter L. Gordon, Jr.

title_tesim: Party at photographer George Cutler's house, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: J. Cullen Fentress was the owner of Fentress Press, and  served as the founding President of the Western States Golf Association.
Dr. Lois Evans was a chiropodist.
Bessie M. Gant was a columnist from the Pittsburgh Courier.
Maudelle Richardson was the wife of H. Leonard Richardson and an avid golfer.
Herman Hill was a newspaper reporter and helped to establish the Pittsburg Courier in Los Angeles. He was also the first African American to play varsity basketball at USC.
Gladys Hill, married to Herman Hill, was  active in civic and social clubs including the Kansis City Brunch Club.
Lillian ("Lil") Fentress was active in the Zeta Phi Beta sorority, the YWCA, and in the Los Angeles Elks organization. She was married to J. Cullen Fentress.
George Cutler was a commercial photographer.

Chester L. Washington became the first African American news employee at the Los Angeles Mirror-News in 1955, and in 1962 he went to work for The Los Angeles Sentinel, where he became editor. He eventually purchased a number of newspapers, becoming the owner of Central News-Wave Publications.

summary_tesim: Dr. William Goodloe was a physician from Washington D.C. who later served more than 2 years in prison for performing an abortion, as well as obstructing justice and bribery charges.
Clarence Muse was an African American vaudevillian and actor.
B.B. Bratton earned his B.A. from the University of Chicago and worked in Lousiana as a certified public accountant. He passed the bar in Los Angeles in 1924 and practiced law.
Dr. Frank Gary Zetar was a chiropractor.
Sidney Dones was a real estate broker and businessman.  He was the first black graduate of LaSalle Extension School, a school that put out legal course materials for people to study at home.

title_tesim: Lillian Randolph and Nina Mae McKinney in Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Nina Mae McKinney was one of the earliest African American leading actresses. Director King Vidor tapped her for the lead in the 1929 musical Hallelujah; however, during that era, Hollywood was reluctant to showcase African American actresses in leading roles.

Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was born in Knoxville, Tennessee, the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (who went on to become an actress). Lillian's best known role was the maid Birdie Lee Coggins on the Great Gildersleeve, which began as a radio program and moved to television. In the 1940s, she and her husband, boxer Jack Chase, purchased a home in the "Sugar Hill" district (West Adams), but could not move in because of restrictive racial covenants. The US Supreme Court declared such covenants unconstitutional in 1948.

Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (who went on to become an actress). In the 1940s, she and her husband, boxer Jack Chase, purchased a home in the "Sugar Hill" district (West Adams), but could not move in because of restrictive racial covenants. The US Supreme Court declared such covenants unconstitutional in 1948.
Jack Chase was a West Coast fighter. He was married to actress Lillian Randolph Chase.

Clarence Muse was an African American vaudevillian, actor, screenwriter, director, composer, and lawyer. Muse was the first African American to "star" in a film. He acted for more than sixty years, and appeared in more than 150 movies.

summary_tesim: Dorothy Vena Johnson was a poet and educator. With Juanita Miller, a social worker and wife of civil rights attorney Loren Miller, she founded the League of Allied Arts in 1939.

Ursula P. Murrell was a pharmacist for 14 years. She then received a bachelor's degree and a master's degree at the University of Southern California. She also did post-graduate work at U.C. Berkeley and UCLA. She was a special music teacher in the Los Angeles Unified School District for 30 years. She was awarded the Honorary PTA Life Membership, the Life Membership in the Los Angeles City Elementary School Music Association 24th Street Elementary School Award, and the Golden Apple at the 1971 Teacher Remembrance Day Award as Outstanding Teacher.  She was also very active in civic organizations.
Dorothy Vena Johnson was a poet and educator.
Sarah Griffin was the wife of dentist Tom Griffin.

Loren Miller was an attorney. He later became a California Superior Court Justice in Los Angeles County. Miller was known for his work fighting for equal access to housing and education. He was also the editor of the California Eagle after Charlotta Bass.

Helen (Duncan) Garrott is fourth from the right, in a group of women closer to the photograph. Ursula (Pruitt) Murrell is at the far right of the photograph. The others in the photograph are unidentified.

summary_tesim: Luther M. Hudson was a funeral director and one of the original founders of the Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company.
Mrs. Damon Lee was the assistant to the Somervilles of the Somerville Hotel (later the Dunbar Hotel).
Wesley J. Fairchild operated the Fairchild Realty Co. after running a laundry.
"Jay" Fairchild was the wife of Wesley J. Fairchild, who operated the Fairchild Realty Co. after running a laundry.

Lillian Tyler was a teacher, was a member of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, and was active in social affairs and civic organizations including the 20-13 Club. She was married to attorney Willis O. Tyler.

Norman O. Houston helped William Nickerson, Jr. to found the Golden State Mutual Life Insruance Company in1925,  and became president in 1945. At one point, Golden State Mutual Life Insurance was the largest black-owned business west of the Mississippi. He expanded the activities of the company over the next 25 years. In 1970 his two sons, Norman B. Houston and Ivan J. Houston, took over active management of the company. He was also first African American to be appointed to the California State Athletic Commission and later served as chairman.

Seated, from L: "Jay" Fairchild (1st, in print dress), Vada Somerville (2nd), Edith Banks (Pruett?) (6th).

"Pegga" Hawkins (nee Smith) was the wife of Congressman Augustus Hawkins. A California native, she moved to Los Angeles in 1936. There, she organized the Women's Sunday Morning Breakfast Club, a charitable organization. She remained active in both charitable and Democratic organizations.
Emily Brown Childress Portwig was well-known for her civic work in Los Angeles and nationally. She attended UCLA and Howard University, and conducted graduate work in bacteriology at USC.
Laura Fowler was well-known for her civic work in Los Angles. She was married to the physician, Dr. Jefferson D. Fowler.

title_tesim: Lena Horne and C. W. Hill, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Lena Horne was an American jazz and pop singer, dancer and actress, and civil rights activist.

Dr. Charles Wesley Hill moved to Los Angeles from Arkansas. Initially working as a dentist, he became a successful businessman and real estate broker. Hill was active in the NAACP,  the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG) where he was co-vice president along with Lena Horne, and other civil rights organizations. He was a member of the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity. Hill was also a fund raiser and party host at his Hollywood home. According to Walter L. Gordon, Jr., at some point Mr. Hill simply disappeared from the Los Angeles scene, and no one knew what had become of him. Census records indicate that he died in Evansville, Indiana in 1977.

title_tesim: Journalists Charlotta Bass, Jessie Mae Brown Beavers, and others, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Jessie Mae (Brown) Beavers was a pioneering black journalist, who began her career at the California Eagle, moving to the Los Angeles Sentinel in 1948. She was a Los Angeles native, who graduated from Los Angeles High School and studied sociology at UCLA. She was a life member of the NAACP, and helped to organize local chapters of the National Association of Media Women and the Black Women's Forum. She also served for 16 years on the Los Angeles Human Relations Commission. She was married to Leroy Albert Beavers Sr.

title_tesim: African American business and legal figures, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: William Nickerson Jr. was a prominent Los Angeles-based businessman and founder of Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company, which at one time was the largest black-owned business west of the Mississippi.

H. A. Howard was a business entrepreneur who founded and was the president of the Broadway Federal Savings and Loan Association. He was very active in civic organizations and was a vice president of the local chapter of the NAACP.
Edgar J. Johnson rose from agent to president of the Golden State Mutual Insurance Company.
George Cannady started out as a firefighter, at a time when African American firefighters were rare. Later he went into the real estate business, and later still he became a lawyer. He died in 1968.
Bernard C. Herndon who moved to Los Angeles around 1940. He was a real estate broker and president of the non-profit Stovall Foundation for community health and welfare

(C.) Albert (Al) Maddox founded the Maddox Realty Company, the Maddox Construction Company, was a co-founded Broadway Federal Savings and Loan Association, and the Chairman of the Board of Directors of the Golden West Real Estate Board. He also participated in numerous civic organizations and was a lifetime member of the NAACP.

Loren Miller (1903-1967) was an attorney in the 1940s. he later became a California Superior Court Justice in Los Angeles County. Miller was known for his work fighting for equal access to housing and education. He was also the editor of the California Eagle after Charlotta Bass.

title_tesim: Police officers Kenneth (Kenny) Harris and Red Davis, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Kenneth Harris was a well known police officer and athlete on the east side of Los Angeles. Harris and Red Davis attended Wadsworth Elementary school together, and remained friends; as adults they were partners on the police force for many years.

Red (Tom or Tommy) Davis was a well known police officer and athlete on the east side of Los Angeles. Davis was a champion baseball and basketball player at Jefferson, and also played in the Negro leagues during the Depression; later he was the police handball champion 12 times over. Davis and Kenneth (Kenny) Harris attended Wadsworth Elementary school together, and remained friends; as adults they were partners on the police force for many years.

title_tesim: Soprano Anne Brown, tenor George Garner, pianist Netta Garner, and others, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Dr. C. W. Hill had a varied, but successful, career as a dentist and real estate broker; he was also involved with the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG), an organization dedicated to protecting the rights of minorities in the entertainment world

Ursula P. Murrell was a pharmacist for 14 years. She then received a bachelor's degree and a master's degree at the University of Southern California. She also did post-graduate work at U.C. Berkeley and UCLA. She was a special music teacher in the Los Angeles Unified School District for 30 years. She was awarded the Honorary PTA Life Membership, the Life Membership in the Los Angeles City Elementary School Music Association 24th Street Elementary School Award, and the Golden Apple at the 1971 Teacher Remembrance Day Award as Outstanding Teacher.  She was also very active in civic organizations.
George Garner was a tenor vocalist (and first African American to sing with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra) and conductor.
Clothilde Woodard was active in Alpha Kappa Alpha (AKA) sorority activities, as well as in the Q-ettes (Wives of Omega Men) organization.
Norma Green was the wife of actor Eddie Green. Mrs. Green was a singer, and she played the piano and violin. The couple had a daughter, Elva Diane.

Portia Griffith taught at Tuskegee Institute and worked for the Los Angeles Unified School District for 26 years. She was a founding member of USC’s Upsilon Chapter of Delta Sigma Theta sorority, and of the Dilettante Bridge Club, the A.D.D. Club, Belle Lettres, and Friendly Educators. She was married to attorney Tom Griffith.

Ruth Lee Towles was a milliner from Chicago, who may have settled in Los Angeles around 1950. She was a stylist for Marva Louis and other fashionable women. Society columns note that she was also a pianist who played at social events.
Netta Paullyn (aka Pauline) Garner was a concert pianist, married to George Garner.
Ethel Sissle (1915-1984) was maried to the band leader Noble Sissle, and later to Walter L. Gordon, Jr.

Tony Hill was the owner of the Tony Hill Ceramics Workshop, located at 3121 W. Jefferson Blvd. for 31 years. He received his AB in Social Work from the University of Chicago; did graduate work in sociology at Columbia University, New York School of Social Work; and was the first African American to study art and ceramics with Glen Lukens at the University of Southern California. He died of a heart attack, and was buried, in Cairo, Egypt during a trip to the far east.
Mitchell Miles was a wealthy man from Buffalo, NY. He and his brother ran a gambling business, but they were pushed out of Buffalo by the mob. They eventually moved to Los Angeles.
Mabel Miles was the wife of Mitchell Miles.

Anne Brown was an African American soprano radio and concert singer, perhaps best known for singing the part of Bess in the world premier of Porgy and Bess in Boston. In the late 1940s, Brown moved to Norway, to escape the continuing racial prejudice in the United States.

title_tesim: Nat King Cole, Noble Sissle, and others, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Paul Leroy "Ox Blood" Howard (1895-1980, Los Angeles) was an American jazz saxophonist and clarinetist.
Maria Cole was a jazz singer who worked with Count Basie and Duke Ellington, among others. She  married to Nat King Cole in 1948.

Dr. N. Curtis King was a physician who founded the Rose Netta Hospital in Los Angeles. It was known for having an interracial staff, and the first interracial blood bank, established by the Red Cross in 1942. Dr. King was also head of the Los Angeles Venereal Clinic and several other clinics.
Curtis C. Taylor was a successful   attorney. He earned his law degree at Howard University .

Noble Lee Sissle was an African-American jazz composer, lyricist, bandleader, singer, and playwright, best known for the Broadway musical "Shuffle Along" (1921), and its hit song I'm Just Wild About Harry.
Sonny Howard was a well known "sportsman" (gambler) on Central Avenue.
Ethel Sissle (1915-1984) was maried to the band leader Noble Sissle, and later to Walter L. Gordon, Jr.
H. H. Towles was a prominent physician and pioneer director of Golden State Mutual Life.

title_tesim: African American business figures, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Dr. William Goodloe was a physician from Washington D.C. who later served more than 2 years in prison for performing an abortion, as well as obstructing justice and bribery charges.
Juanita Goodloe was the wife of Dr. William A. Goodloe. She was an ex-chorus girl with Ethel Sissle Gordon, Walter L. Gordon, Jr.'s wife.
Mitchell Miles was a wealthy man from Buffalo, NY. He and his brother ran a gambling business, but they were pushed out of Buffalo by the mob. They eventually moved to Los Angeles.

Horace P. Clark, Sr. from Mississsippi, graduated from Alcorn College. He relocated to Chicago and became a railroad man. He moved to Los Angeles where he and his first wife, Vera, built the Clark Hotel.
Clarence Muse was an African American vaudevillian and actor.
William Nickerson, Jr. was the founder of Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company.
Albert (Al) Maddox was a real estate broker.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Seated at table, left to right: Mitchell Miles, Mabel Miles, Dr. William A. Goodloe and Juanita Goodloe, and an unidentified man. Standing in rear, left to right: Horace Clark, Albert (Al) Maddox, William Nickerson, Clarence Muse, and an unidentified man (in waiter's costume).
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection

summary_tesim: LaTanya Griffin (or "L'Tanya," or "L'Tanya Martinique") was a society woman, a model and a fashion designer. In her early twenties, she was awarded a contract by the Guild Studios to design gowns for Ida Lapino.  She was contracted to with the Edward D. Wood Production company to design gowns for 20 of its films in 1954, making her the first African American designer to have a contract with a Hollywood film studio. She and Earl Griffin divorced in 1949.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: LaTanya (L'Tanya) Griffin inscribed this photo to Walter L. Gordon, Jr.: "To the 'Great One': It is my pleasure knowing you. -- L'Tanya Griffin. At the bottom of the photo, Walter L. Gordon, Jr. has inscribed the photo as "Red Griffin's Pygmalion" (Red Griffin was LaTanya's husband).
All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

summary_tesim: Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (also an actress). Lillian's best known role was the maid Birdie Lee Coggins on the Great Gildersleeve, a radio program that moved to television.
Louise Beavers was a well known African was a member of the Sigma Gamma Rho sorority and in 1976 was posthumously inducted into the Black Filmmakers Hall of Fame.

Lillian Cumber (Victoria Lillian Fisher)  started as a secretary for Walter L. Gordon, was a newspaper columnist for 25 years, and in 1956 founded the Lil Cumber Attraction Agency for African American actors. She was the first African American woman to represent actors in the Motion Picture and Television industry.
George Garner was a tenor vocalist and conductor. He was the first African American to sing with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra.

Ben Carter was an African American character actor on stage, screen, and radio. He often worked as a straight man with Mantan Moreland in vaudeville routines. He acted in many movies, often uncredited, including Maryland (1940) and Reap the Wild Wind (1942). He died in New York in either 1946 or 1947.
Hattie McDaniel was the first African American actress to win an Academy Award, an Oscar for Best Supporting Actress as Mammy in Gone with the Wind.
Herman Spurlock was known for putting together shows and entertainment for nightclubs and fundraisers. He was well known on Central Avenue.
Eddie Greenwas an African American  comedian who performed with the Billy Minsky burlesque show for about five years and played the character Eddie the waiter on the radio program Duffy's Tavern.

title_tesim: Phi Beta Sigma men, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Mr. Gordon Sr. was Walter L. Gordon, Jr.'s father.
B.B. Bratton earned his B.A. from the University of Chicago and worked in Lousiana as a certified public accountant. He passed the bar in Los Angeles in 1924 and practiced law.
Dr. Frank Gary Zetar was a chiropractor.
S.P. Johnson was a director at the Conner-Johnson Mortuary.

Horace P. Clark, Sr. was originally from Mississsippi and graduated from Alcorn College. He relocated to Chicago for better job opportunities and became a railroad man. He later moved to Los Angeles where he and his first wife, Vera, built the Clark Hotel.
Stokes was indentified as a pharmacist assistant.

Dr. Charles Wesley Hill moved to Los Angeles from Arkansas. Initially working as a dentist, he became a uccessful businessman and real estate broker. Hill was active in the NAACP,  the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG) where he was co-vice president along with Lena Horne, and other civil rights organizations. He was a member of the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity. Hill was also a fund raiser and party host at his Hollywood home. According to Walter L. Gordon, Jr., at some point Mr. Hill simply disappeared from the Los Angeles scene, and no one knew what had become of him. Census records indicate that he died in Evansville, Indiana in 1977.
Afue McDowell was a well-known attorney.

title_tesim: Jim Goodrich (?), Leon Washington, Eddie Heywood, and Louie Cole, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Eddie Heywood was a jazz pianist, arranger and bandleader.

Jim (James Russell) Goodrich had a rich and varied journalism career. He obtained a journalism degree from the University of Illinois, and earned two masters degrees from UCLA: in journalism and political science. He was an urban affairs reporter for the Press-Telegram (in Long Beach), and an assistant director of information and reseatch for the Joint Center of Political Studies in Washington D.C.. He was with John son Publications for more than 10 years where he was, at various times, Hollywood editor and associate editor of Ebony magazine and West Coast Bureau chief for Jet magazine. He was the public relationist for Sammy Davis Jr. and, afterwards, as an editor at the Los Angeles Sentinel (1966-67).  He went on to teach at Harbor College, UCLA, and UC  Irvine, then became the director of the Mass Media Program at Central Washington University in Washington state.
Leon Washington, Jr. was the founder and publisher of the Los Angeles Sentinel.
Louis "Louie" V. Cole began his career as a newspaper reporter for the Sentinel and became a civic leader of South Central Los Angeles. He dubbed himself the "Mayor of Central Avenue."
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Jim Goodrich is the likely identity of the man standing on the far left. Leon Washington is the second man from the left (in army uniform). Eddie Heywood is second from the right (in checked jacket), and Louie Cole is on the far right. The two women in the photograph are unidentified.

title_tesim: Dancers Charles "Honi" Coles, Charles "Cholly" Atkins, Mae Johnson, Alyce Key, and Pete Nugent, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Charles “Cholly” Atkins (1913–2003) was an American jazz tap dancer and vaudeville performer, who later became noted as the house choreographer for the various artists on the Motown label. (Wikipedia)

Gary Lambert “Pete” Nugent was a preeminent tap dancer in the class act tradition. In the 1930s he was a member of the act of “Pete, Peaches and Duke,” performing in venues such as the Cotton Club. After that he had a solo act “Public Tapper, Number One”.  During World War II he spent his military service touring in Irving Berlin’s show This is the Army. Although Nugent considered himself a tap dancer first and foremost, he preferred “all body motion and no tap”, rather than “all tap and no body motion”. Later, Pete teamed up with Charles “Honi” Coles and opened a dance studio in NYC with called “Dance Craft.” He also performed with Coles in “A History of Tap Dance and its Relationship to Jazz” at the  Newport Jazz Festival, which brought tap back into the public eye.

Mae Johnson was a showgirl, dancer, singer and actress who was well known in the black entertainment world in the 1930s and 1940s. Her career began with nightclub performances  in New York. She had the staring role in the film "Keep Punching." On the west coast she appeared in a few Hollywood films that had black musical numbers, giving a memorable performance in Lena Horne's "Stormy Weather." She recorded some songs with Austin McCoy and co-starred in the hit play "Two in a bed" with Louise Beavers.
Alice (Alyce) Key was a Los Angeles dancer who later became a journalist, community activist, and political leader.

Charles "Honi" Coles was an American actor and tap dancer known for his extraordinary elegance and technical precision. He learned to dance on the streets of Philadelphia and went on to a career on stage in vaudeville and the concert world, in film, and on television. He served as president of the Negro Actors Guild and was at the forefront of the tap revival starting in the early 1970s. He brought tap to the world of concert dance with his performance in the Joffrey Ballet production of Agnes De Mille's "Conversations on the Dance", in 1978. In 1983 he received the Tony Award, Fred Astaire Award, and Drama Desk Award for best featured actor and dancer in a musical for the Broadway hit, My One and Only.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

At table, left to right: Charles "Cholly" Atkins, Charles "Hone" Coles, Mae Johnson, unidentified woman, and Pete Nugent. Alyce Key is standing behind the couple at the piano, with a man's hand resting on her shoulder. Atkins and Coles, preeminent tap dancers, performed together for nineteen years, starting in 1940. Atkins was an expert wing dancer, while Coles's specialty was precision.
The man at the far right of the photo was identified by Mr. Gordon as Langston Hughes; however, there is a handwritten caption identifying him as Peter Nugent Deacon(?).

title_tesim: Actors and activists in Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Doris Young Houston was the wife of Norman O. Houston, co-founder of the Golden State Insurance Company.
Joseph Scott was an attorney and Catholic layperson.

Louise Beavers was a well known African American film and television actress. She was a member of the Sigma Gamma Rho sorority and in 1976 was posthumously inducted into the Black Filmmakers Hall of Fame.
Louis, known as the Brown Bomber, was the world heavyweight boxing champion from 1937 to 1949.

Norman O. Houston helped William Nickerson, Jr. to found the Golden State Mutual Life Insruance Company in1925,  and became president in 1945. At one point, Golden State Mutual Life Insurance was the largest black-owned business west of the Mississippi. He expanded the activities of the company over the next 25 years. In 1970 his two sons, Norman B. Houston and Ivan J. Houston, took over active management of the company. He was also first African American to be appointed to the California State Athletic Commission and later served as chairman.
Randolph Scott was an actor.
Adolphe Menjou was a French-American actor.

Betty Hill was born in Nashville, Tennessee, and moved to Los Angeles in 1912 or 1913. Dubbed by the Los Angeles Sentinel the "Mother of Negro Political Leaders," Hill founded the Women's Republican Study Club (later the Women's Political Study Club) in Los Angeles in 1929. (It eventually expanded throughout the state.) Her many causes included fighting Jim Crow in Los Angeles hospitals and public swimming pools, abolishing separate civil service lists for African American school teachers, and getting the Board of Education to approve a child care center program. Hill was married to Sgt. Abraham Hill, who died in 1948.

He was also the first African American to play on the U.S. Olympic basketball team, in the 1948 Summer Olympics. He later went on to play basketball in the NBA, with the Baltimore Bullets and Boston Celtics.

title_tesim: Scene at the Club Alabam, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Iva Washington was one of the first African Americans to teach in the Los Angeles Unified School District. She attended Los Angeles High School and the University of Southern California, where she earned a teaching credential. For most of her career she taught at the 36th Street School in LAUSD.

Elizabeth Estelle Fry Vaughn was the first wife of architect Ralph Vaughn. They had one son, Ronald Vaughn. Elizabeth was a school librarian in Los Angeles for many years. She passed away in Stockton, CA in August 1990 at the age of 78.
Ralph Augustine Vaughn was an architect, licensed interior designer and real estate broker. He lived in Los Angeles from 1938-1998. He died in October 2000 in Stockton, CA.

Charles L. Ennis was a dentist and one of the first African Americans to graduate from the USC School of Dentistry. He was a member of the Kappa Alpha Psi fraternity, a member of the NAACO, and the USC Century Club and supported numerous causes.
Leon Washington, Jr. was the founder and publisher of the Los Angeles Sentinel.

Loren Miller was an attorney at the time; he later became a California Superior Court Justice in Los Angeles County. Miller was known for his work fighting for equal access to housing and education. He was also the editor of the California Eagle after Charlotta Bass.

Dynamite Jackson (born Ernest Bendy) won the California state heavyweight title, before going on to become the first African American boxing referee in the state. He was also the proprietor of a tavern on Central Avenue that bore his name (it eventually moved to West Adams, near Crenshaw). He died in 1967, at the age of 55.

title_tesim: Charlotta Bass, owner and publisher of the California Eagle, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Charlotta A. Bass was the owner and publisher of Los Angeles' oldest black newspaper, the California Eagle, from 1912 to 1951. The newspaper, which began publishing in 1879, was one of the longest-running African American newspapers in the West. Bass was also a pioneering civil rights activist and erstwhile political candidate. She organized the South Los Angeles Protective League, to deal with the ousting of non-white families from all-white neighborhoods; the League went on to become one of the main organizations organizing against restrictive racial covenants in Los Angeles. She walked in picket lines to integrate the aircraft industry, among others.  Bass original came to California from Rhode Island as a very young woman, and married J. B. Bass, then the owner of the California Eagle. She retired in 1959, selling the paper to Judge Loren Miller. She died in 1969.

Strode’s film career includes important roles in Spartacus (1960); Sergeant Rutledge (1960); The Professionals (1966), and; Once Upon a Time in the West (1968).

title_tesim: Picnic party near E. 109th Street and Central Avenue, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr. was an African American attorney in Los Angeles.
Louise Brooks was in charge of the kitchens at clubs such as the Dunbar Grill and the F-Fortune Club.
Brice Union Taylor, who was born without a left hand, was an offensive guard for the USC Trojans football team. He was selected as an All-American in 1925.
Mitchell Miles was a wealthy man from Buffalo, NY. He and his brother ran a gambling business, but they were pushed out of Buffalo by the mob. They eventually moved to Los Angeles.

Marshall Miles was a businessman who helped manage the boxing career of Joe Louis remained his lifelong friend and adviser. Marshall and his brothers, Percy and Mitchell, were among the pioneering African-American businessmen in Buffalo in the 1920's and 1930's, beginning with a shoe-repair shop, then with Miles Brothers Dairy. In addition to managing boxers, Miles branched out into real estate and investments.

Norman O. Houston helped William Nickerson, Jr. to found the Golden State Mutual Life Insruance Company in1925,  and became president in 1945. At one point, Golden State Mutual Life Insurance was the largest black-owned business west of the Mississippi. He expanded the activities of the company over the next 25 years. In 1970 his two sons, Norman B. Houston and Ivan J. Houston, took over active management of the company. He was also first African American to be appointed to the California State Athletic Commission and later served as chairman.

summary_tesim: Betty Hill, dubbed by the Los Angeles Sentinel the "Mother of Negro Political Leaders," Hill founded the Women's Republican Study Club (later the Women's Political Study Club) in Los Angeles in 1929. Her many causes included fighting Jim Crow in Los Angeles hospitals and public swimming pools, abolishing separate civil service lists for African American school teachers, and getting the Board of Education to approve a child care center program. Emily Woodie was very active in sorority affairs. She was also a school principal.

Attorney Martha Jefferson Louis was married a few times. She was first married to a minister named Percy Williams. Her second husband was attorney Bernard Jefferson. She later married boxer Joe Louis. She died in 1991 and is buried at Arlington National Cemetery with Joe Louis.

Dr. Vada Somerville was the wife of Dr. John Sommerville. Like her husband, she was a graduate of the USC Dental School (she became the dental school's second black graduate). She and her husband established the Dunbar Hotel in Los Angeles.

In 1940s coalition struggles to achieve fair housing, open hiring in the defense industry, and economic independence, Rev. Russell was in the forefront.
