title_tesim: Birthday Party for attorney Curtis Taylor, at the Broady residence, Beverly Hills, 1965
summary_tesim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr. was an African American attorney in Los Angeles.

Yvonne Brathwaite Burke is a politician from Los Angeles who served as a member of the U.S. House of Representatives (1973-1979) and as a member of the Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors (1979-1980; 1992-2008).

Elbert T. Hudson was the son of the civil rights leader H. Claude Hudson. He graduated from UCLA and received his law degree from Loyola University. He was a fighter pilot with the Tuskegee Airmen during World War II. He was the first African American to be named president of the LosAngeles Police Commission in 1966 and took the position as head of the L.A. chapter of the NAACP in 1971. In 1972, he took over the Broadway Federal Bank, founded by his Father. Under his guidance, the bank supported growth in minority neighborhoods.
Sherman W. Smith Sr. was a municipal and superior court judge in Los Angeles during the 1960s and 1975, retiring in 1975.

Loren Miller was an attorney in the 1940s. He later became a California Superior Court Justice in Los Angeles County. Miller was known for his work fighting for equal access to housing and education. He was also the editor of the California Eagle after Charlotta Bass.
Earl C. Broady started his career as a janitor and became a judge on the Los Angeles County Superior Court.
Roscoe C. Carroll was an attorney and a Los Angeles civic leader. He was preseident of the Langston Law Club.

title_tesim: Attorney Ivan Johnson addressing an audience, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Following a career as a Superior Court judge in Los Angeles, Goodwin (Goodie) Jess Knight was Lieutenant Governor of California from 1946 to 1953, and Governor of California from 1953 to 1959.
Bratton was a candidate for Los Angeles City Council and had been a member of the Phi Beta Sigma.

Vince Monroe Townsend, Jr. was an attorney. Admitted to the California Bar in 1943, he was an advocate for abolishing restrictive covenants for minority groups on real estate in residential areas in the late 1940s. From about 1947 to about 1965, he ran for political office at the city and State levels several times. Affiliated with the Republican Party starting in 1956, he returned to the Democratic Party in 1966.  As of 1966, he was a Los Angeles County public defender. In 1987 he was a judge pro tempore in the Inglewood Municipal Court. Townsend is listed as an actor in three films: Valley of the Head Hunters (1953), Jungle Man-Eaters (1954), and Weird Science (1985).

Ivan J. Johnson III was a Los Angeles attorney. He was one of the first African Americans to letter in track at USC and UC Berkeley. He was a charter member of the Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity. Shortly before his death, he was honored by the Langston Law Club as the dean of  local attorneys.
Jessie Graves was actor and member of Hollywood Screen Actor's Guild. He was known for often playing the role of an austere butler.

Xenophon F. Lang was a Los Angeles Municipal Court judge. At the start of his law career he was Walter Gordon's professional colleague.
Lovell Moore was a real estate broker.

title_tesim: Norman O. Houston, Joe Louis, Charles Matthews, and Fred Roberts, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Charles Hearde Matthews, Sr. graduated with honors from the Boalt Hall School of Law at UC Berkeley in 1930,  entered the Los Angeles district attorney's office. Matthews was twice denied admission to the Bar Association, at a time when the Association excluded African Americans. (The Bar Association eliminated racial barriers in 1950.) He eventually rose to a key position in charge of the Master Calendar Department of the Superior Court. During this period, Matthews also served as a member of the Los Angeles City Police Commission. He returned to private practice in 1945. Among the many boards and civic organizations on which Matthews served were those of the Los Angeles Metropolitan YMCA, the Los Angeles Chapter of the NAACP, the Golden State Mutual Life Insurance company, and the Liberty Savings and Loan Association.

Norman O. Houston helped William Nickerson, Jr. to found the Golden State Mutual Life Insruance Company in1925,  and became president in 1945. At one point, Golden State Mutual Life Insurance was the largest black-owned business west of the Mississippi. He expanded the activities of the company over the next 25 years. In 1970 his two sons, Norman B. Houston and Ivan J. Houston, took over active management of the company. He was also first African American to be appointed to the California State Athletic Commission and later served as chairman.

Frederick Roberts, the first African American elected to the California Assembly, represented a district that was 70% white from 1918–1934 when he was defeated by the New Dealer, a young man named Augustus Hawkins. The Roberts family owned a mortuary in which Frederick was employed; he was also the publisher of the New Age newspaper. The City of Los Angeles named a park after him.
Joe Louis, known as the Brown Bomber, was the world heavyweight boxing champion from 1937 to 1949.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
L to R: Norman O. Houston, Joe Louis, Charles Matthews, Fred Roberts.

title_tesim: Vince Monroe Townsend, Joe Saunders, Muriel Andrade, and Rayfield Lundy, Los Angeles 1940s
summary_tesim: Joe Saunders was an attorney. Mr. Gordon stated that Joe Saunders was not a Los Angeles attorney.

Vince Monroe Townsend, Jr. was an attorney. Admitted to the California Bar in 1943, he was an advocate for abolishing restrictive covenants for minority groups on real estate in residential areas in the late 1940s. From about 1947 to about 1965, he ran for political office at the city and State levels several times. Affiliated with the Republican Party starting in 1956, he returned to the Democratic Party in 1966.  As of 1966, he was a Los Angeles County public defender. In 1987 he was a judge pro tempore in the Inglewood Municipal Court. Townsend is listed as an actor in three films: Valley of the Head Hunters (1953), Jungle Man-Eaters (1954), and Weird Science (1985).

Muriel Andrade was born in Martha's Vineyard, and in the 1940s was introduced to and eventually engaged to the heavyweight boxing champion Joe Louis (though she eventually married Ulysses Lear). Her mother was Evelyn DeLoach, secretary to Leverett Saltonstall, governor of Massachusetts from 1939-1945.

Rayfield Lundy was an attorney.  He was a staunch Republican and became one of their minor officers in the party. He lost his license due to a dispute that involved a client who claimed to have paid Lundy a sum of money, which Lundy denied.

Rayfield Lundy was an attorney. He received his BA degree from Howard University and attended Howard University Law school on a scholarship. He was a staunch Republican and became one of their minor officers in the party. At one point, he lost his license due to a dispute that involved a client who claimed to have paid Lundy a sum of money, which Lundy denied. But he was still a practicing lawyer in the late 1950s. He ran unsuccissfully for the state Legislature, representing the 55th Assembly District. He was very active in Willowbrook civic organizations.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

Marque Neal was a Los Angeles city councilman, who at one time was married to Ivie Anderson, a former vocalist for Duke Ellington's orchestra. He also managed a property called the Nealander on Adams, bought for him by Anderson, where people could rent rooms by the day or week.
Photographer Norman Siminoff was known for photographing Central Avenue activities.
Lil Hammond was the ex-wife of Detroit businessman Thomas "Rooster" Hammond. Mrs. Hammond moved to Los Angeles in 1943. She was the proprietor of Lil's Flower Box on west Jefferson.

title_tesim: Allen and Clothilde Woodard, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Allen C. Woodard, III, attended UCLA and Southwestern Law School in Los Angeles. Once he received his real estate broker's license, he became active in fighting racial restrictions on property ownership and occupation. He also served as President of West View Hospital Organization, supporting the efforts of African American physicians seeking to work at Los Angeles hospitals. He was active in many community organizations, such as the NAACP and YMCA, and also an active member of the Omega Psi Phi Fraternity. He died in 1984.
Clothilde Woodard was active in Alpha Kappa Alpha (AKA) sorority activities, as well as in the Q-ettes (Wives of Omega Men) organization.

summary_tesim: Monte Hawley was one of the most successful African American actors of his time. He got his start in Chicago with the Lafayette Players. From there he moved to New York, where he was a successful stage actor before moving to Hollywood where he found work in motion pictures. Hawley was born in Chicago in 1901, and died in New York City in 1950.

title_tesim: African American actors, Los Angeles, 1940s (2nd photo)
summary_tesim: Jack Chase was married to actress Lillian Randolph Chase.
George Garner was a tenor vocalist (and first African American to sing with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra) and conductor.
Norma Green was the wife of actor Eddie Green. Mrs. Green was a singer, and she played the piano and violin. The couple had a daughter, Elva Diane.
Eddie Green played the character Eddie the waiter on the radio program Duffy's Tavern.

Lillian Cumber (Victoria Lillian Fisher)  started as a secretary for Walter L. Gordon, was a newspaper columnist for 25 years, and in 1956 founded the Lil Cumber Attraction Agency for African American actors. She was the first African American woman to represent actors in the Motion Picture and Television industry.

Louise Beavers was a well known African American film and television actress. She was a member of the Sigma Gamma Rho sorority and in 1976 was posthumously inducted into the Black Filmmakers Hall of Fame.

Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (who went on to become an actress). In the 1940s, she and her husband, boxer Jack Chase, purchased a home in the "Sugar Hill" district (West Adams), but could not move in because of restrictive racial covenants. The US Supreme Court declared such covenants unconstitutional in 1948.

Ben Carter was an African American character actor on stage, screen, and radio. He often worked as a straight man with Mantan Moreland in vaudeville routines. He acted in many movies, often uncredited, including Maryland (1940) and Reap the Wild Wind (1942). He died in New York in either 1946 or 1947.

Dr. Charles Wesley Hill moved to Los Angeles from Arkansas. Initially working as a dentist, he became a uccessful businessman and real estate broker. Hill was active in the NAACP,  the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG) where he was co-vice president along with Lena Horne, and other civil rights organizations. He was a member of the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity. Hill was also a fund raiser and party host at his Hollywood home. According to Walter L. Gordon, Jr., at some point Mr. Hill simply disappeared from the Los Angeles scene, and no one knew what had become of him. Census records indicate that he died in Evansville, Indiana in 1977.
Hattie McDaniel was the first African American actress to win an Academy Award, an Oscar for Best Supporting Actress as Mammy in Gone with the Wind.
Herman Spurlock, well known on Central Avenue, made a living putting together shows and entertainment for nightclubs and fundraisers.

title_tesim: Affair at Club Alabam, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Prince Spencer was an African American tap dancer, who was born in 1917 in South Carolina. In 1941, he joined the dance troupe the Four Step Brothers.
Tillie Euliss was a socialite known for her hats.
Juliette Ball was a showgirl and actress.

Clarence Moore was the proprietor of the Last Round and Club Memo on Central Avenue. He also started a semi-professional baseball team called the Clarence Moore's All-Stars in 1934 as a means to launch baseball careers for local youths.

Elihu "Black Dot" McGee was a night club owner and sportsman. He owned establishments around Central Avenue, including the Turf Record Shop, the Sportsman's Club, The Casa blanca and The Downbeat (co-owned).

Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (who went on to become an actress). In the 1940s, she and her husband, boxer Jack Chase, purchased a home in the "Sugar Hill" district (West Adams), but could not move in because of restrictive racial covenants. The US Supreme Court declared such covenants unconstitutional in 1948.
Lil Hammond was the ex-wife of Detroit businessman Thomas "Rooster" Hammond. Mrs. Hammond moved to Los Angeles in 1943. She was the proprietor of Lil's Flower Box on west Jefferson.

Publicist Earl "Red" Griffin was the IFRG's public relations director. He was known for discovering and promoting his eventual wife, LaTanya Griffin.

title_tesim: Elmer Fain, Les Hite, Nat King Cole and Maria Cole, New York City, 1947
summary_tesim: Les Hite was a saxophone musician and bandleader, starting out in New York and relocating to Los Angeles. His big Los Angeles band was called Sebastian's Cotton Club Orchestra. Because he rarely recorded, his work is poorly documented (he recorded some session s bewteen 1940 and 1942). He also ran a talent agency with Elmer Fain.
Nat King Cole was an American popular singer and jazz pianist.

Elmer Fain was a jazz musician and, during the era of segregated bands, the business agent for the the Black Musicians Union. He later became a top talent agent, running an agency with xylophonist and bandleader Les Hite. As the bandleader of Elmer Fain & His Orchestra, he recorded "Bolero at the Savoy," "Pick a Rib," and "Stampede in G Minor" (available in the Big Band Jazz: Junilee Sessions 1943-1946).  He is also credited as an actor in Round-Up Time inTexas (1937), and Hubb's Weekend Trip (1928).
Maria, Nat King Cole's wife, was also known as Maria Hawkins or Maria Hawkins Ellington.

William L. "Billy" Rowe an African American journalist . He was known for his column titled "Billy Rowe's Note Book" in the Pittsburgh Courier, and later worked for the Amsterdam Times in New York City. He headed a public relations firm with boxer Joe Louis, and was the first African-American deputy Police Commissioner in New York. He was also known as an aviator, a fabulous dancer, and was very popular in social circles.

caption_tesim: Handwritten inscription: Alice Keys [i.e. Alyce Key] in a seductive pose."
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Walter L. Gordon, Jr.: "Alyce Key is compared to Helen Forrest. They both dress alike, they look alike, their features are alike, and they pose alike. It's very hard for most people when they look at the two photographs to not be confused as to which one this is. This apparently is Alyce Key. I get a close-up view here, and the features and facial lines make it more likely this is Alyce Key."
Los Angeles journalist Alyce Key photographed reclining on a bed in a dressing gown.

title_tesim: William Goodloe, Juanita Goodloe, Walter Gordon, Jr., Liz McCullough, and Mye Haddox in Los Angeles, circa 1951
summary_tesim: Mye Haddox was a physician from Shreveport, Louisiana.
Juanita Goodloe was the wife of Dr. William A. Goodloe. She was an ex-chorus girl with Ethel Sissle Gordon, Walter L. Gordon, Jr.'s wife.
Liz McCullough came to Los Angeles to visit her boyfriend (he had moved from Chicago to L.A. to start a tombstone business).
Walter L. Gordon, Jr. was an African American attorney in Los Angeles.
Dr. William Goodloe was a physician from Washington D.C. who later served more than 2 years in prison for performing an abortion, as well as obstructing justice and bribery charges.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: ark://21198/zz0025pwqj
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
Left to right: Dr. William A. Goodloe, Juanita Goodloe, Walter Gordon, Jr., Liz McCullough, Mye Haddox.

Walter L. Gordon, Jr.: "Now these were some hot runners. This man [on left] is Dr. Goodloe, lived in Washington DC. He had a yacht. He was an abortionist; all the congressmen used him. Woman to his right was a similar situation to Ethel [Gordon], on chorus line at Billy Rose’s club. This was a black gal, called high yellow. ... In the middle is me. To the right is Lizzy Putnam (Chicago) [a later interview corrected her name to Liz McCullough]. These are hot flyers. Here’s the disgruntled husband. His name is Mye Hubbard [i.e. Haddox]. He was well known in Shreveport Louisiana. His office must have a photograph section, because people would write me to say they’d seen my photo on his office wall. It was like a clique, we all knew each other. You can look at them and tell they were high rollers. Goodloe got sent to the penitentiary. As a matter of fact, he came out here on a kind of sojourn and when he went back he was supposed to go. This was right before that. This one, look at that chin. His wife… He was a worried husband. Hubbard’s wife, not in photo, she had her own coterie of friends and he didn’t like it. He’s half mad in this photograph, doesn’t like the company he’s keeping. This was taken at my office, I believe. Liz Putnam was just a visitor. Hubbard’s wife left him out here, when he went back he went by himself."
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection

title_tesim: Brother Williams, Almena Lomax, Florence Cadrez and Aristide Chapman, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Almena Lomax was born Hallie Almena Davis on July 23, 1915, in Galveston, Texas. She became a veteran journalist and a civil rights activist in Los Angeles, working first at the California Eagle and eventually starting the Los Angeles Tribune. This is where she met her husband, Lucius W. Lomax, Jr., who she married in 1949. Her activism started early; in 1935, she and a group of African American students at Los Angeles City College founded the James Weldon Johnson club "to further the study and advances of Negro students." In 1946, she won the first Wendell L. Willkie award for Negro journalism in the 1940s, and covered civil rights movement in the South during the 1950s and 1960s. Later, Governor Ronald Reagan named her to the state's Coordinating Council for Higher Education. She died on March 25, 2011, at the age of 95.

Florence "Tiny" Cadrez (also known as Cadrez Brantley) was a well-respected musician from Los Angeles. For many years, she served as the Secretary of the Netro Musicians Union (Local 767) on Central Avenue. Later, when that union integrated, she served as the Secretary for the merged Local 47.  She was also the first President of the local National Association of Negro Musicians, later serving as its treasurer for many years. She died in 1985.
Aristide Chapman was the lover of Henry "Brother" Williams.
Henry "Brother" Williams was an openly gay man (his lover was Aristide Chapman) who operated the Brother's Rendezvous underground club.
subject_topic_tesim: African American labor union members--California--Los Angeles
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
L to R: Henry "Brother" Williams, Almena (Davis) Lomax, Florence Cadrez, Aristide Chapman.
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection
combined_subject_ssim: African American labor union members--California--Los Angeles

title_tesim: Norman O. Houston, Edwin Jefferson, and Ivan J. Johnson III at a golf awards ceremony, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Ivan J. Johnson III was a Los Angeles attorney. He was one of the first African Americans to letter in track at USC and UC Berkeley. He was a charter member of the Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity. Shortly before his death, he was honored by the Langston Law Club as the dean of  local attorneys.

Edwin L. Jefferson was the first African American judge west of the Mississippi River, appointed to the Los Angeles Municipal Court (1941), the Los Angeles County Superior Court (1949), and the 2nd District Court of Appeal (1961).

Norman O. Houston was one of the first African Americans to earn a varsity letter in football at UC Berkeley. He graduated from UCLA and became an executive with the Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company. He was appointed president of the Los Angeles NAACP in 1964.  He participated in the 1965 Selma, Alabama March. He was Deputy Assistant Secretary for Administration, U. S. Department of Health, Education and Welfare during the Nixon adminisatration 1971-1973.

summary_tesim: Thomas J. "Tom" Bradley was the 38th Mayor of Los Angeles, serving from 1973 to 1993. He has been the only African-American mayor of Los Angeles, and his 20 years in office mark the longest tenure by any mayor in the city's history.

Red (Tom or Tommy) Davis was a well known police officer and athlete on the east side of Los Angeles. Davis was a champion baseball and basketball player at Jefferson, and also played in the Negro leagues during the Depression; later he was the police handball champion 12 times over.

Kenneth Harris was a well known police officer and athlete on the east side of Los Angeles. Harris and Red Davis attended Wadsworth Elementary school together, and remained friends; as adults they were partners on the police force for many years.

summary_tesim: Wilmer James, one of California’s first African-American designers of commercial ceramics, learned the technique of producing crackle glazes while working for Barbara Willis in North Hollywood. She founded the Ceramics Workshop studio on Jefferson Boulevard (with Tony Hill) in 1944. After the importation of inexpensive European and Japanese ceramics ... rebounded in the late 1950s, many California ceramics manufacturers, including James, went out of business. She went on to become a printmaker, a commercial artist and a prominent arts educator.

title_tesim: Black tie event in Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: LaTanya Griffin (or "L'Tanya," or "L'Tanya Martinique") was a society woman, a model and a fashion designer. In her early twenties, she was awarded a contract by the Guild Studios to design gowns for Ida Lapino.  She was contracted to with the Edward D. Wood Production company to design gowns for 20 of its films in 1954, making her the first African American designer to have a contract with a Hollywood film studio. She and Earl Griffin divorced in 1949.
Camille Cannady, the wife of George Cannady, acted in local theater and wrote an occasional column for the Sentinel called "About people and things."
Dr. John Coleman was a prominent dentist and a member of the Grand Boule Sigma Pi Phi and Alpha Phi Alpha fraternities.
George Cannady started out as a firefighter, at a time when African American firefighters were rare. Later he went into the real estate business, and later still he became a lawyer. He died in 1968.

title_tesim: Melvyn Douglas, Ouida Williams, L'Tanya, David Williams and others at a political event, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: LaTanya Griffin (or "L'Tanya," or "L'Tanya Martinique") was a society woman, a model and a fashion designer. In her early twenties, she was awarded a contract by the Guild Studios to design gowns for Ida Lapino. She was contracted to with the Edward D. Wood Production company to design gowns for 20 of its films in 1954, making her the first African American designer to have a contract with a Hollywood film studio. She and Earl Griffin divorced in 1949.
Melvyn Douglas was an actor and husband of actress and politician Helen Gahagen Douglas.
David W. Williams, who in 1969 became the first African American judge appointed to the Federal Court west of the Mississippi, was part of a second generation of lawyers in Los Angeles.
Ouida Williams was the wife of attorney David W. Williams.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

title_tesim: Constance Baker Motley (?) and her sister, 1940s
summary_tesim: Constance Baker Motley was the first African American woman to serve in the New York state Senate, and the first woman to be a Manhattan borough president. During the 1940s, she held a position with the NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund in New York, where she worked for 20 years. In this capacity, she became one of the first women to argue cases before the Supreme Court (winning 9 out of the 10 cases she argued). In 1966, she was appointed as a judge on the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of New York by President Lyndon B. Johnson.

title_tesim: Mae Johnson, Nelson Creswell, Rex Ingram, Avanelle Harris, Alyce Key, Count Basie and Charlotte Strickland at the Plantation Club, Los Angeles, 1950s
summary_tesim: Rex Ingram was an actor.

Nelson Creswell was the owner of the Cress Bar in 1947, was known as a fastidious dresser and was frequently mentioned in social reporting in the Los Angeles Sentinel. According to Walter Gordon, Jr., he was known as someone who occasionally sold goods door to door, and who never squealed on anyone as to where they got something.
Alice Key was a showgirl who later became a journalist, community activist, and political leader
Avanelle Harris at one time was a dancer in the Cotton Club Cuties chorus line. She also performed with the Mosetti Trio act.  She was married to Annanias, of the Berry Brothers.
Maudine Simmons Creswell may have been married to Nelson Creswell; she answered to either Maudine Simmons or Maudine Creswell.
Charlotte (West) Strickland was the wife of Dr. Strickland of Philadelphia (possibly Wilbur H. Strickland?).

Mae Johnson was a showgirl, dancer, singer and actress who was well known in the black entertainment world in the 1930s and 1940s. Her career began with nightclub performances  in New York. She had the staring role in the film "Keep Punching." On the west coast she appeared in a few Hollywood films that had black musical numbers, giving a memorable performance in Lena Horne's "Stormy Weather." She recorded some songs with Austin McCoy and co-starred in the hit play "Two in a bed" with Louise Beavers.
William "Count" Basie was an American jazz pianist, organist, bandleader and composer.
Catherine Morgan was Count Basie's wife.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: The Plantation Club was located at 108th St. and S. Central Avenue.
All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

title_tesim: Actress Mae Johnson on the opening night of Two in a Bed at the Musart Theatre, Los Angeles, 1946
summary_tesim: Mitchell Miles was a wealthy man from Buffalo, NY. He and his brother ran a gambling business, but they were pushed out of Buffalo by the mob. They eventually moved to Los Angeles.
Millie Monroe Bennett was a showgirl. She was married to Lt. Col. Bob Bennett.
Lt. Col. Bob Bennett was a military man from Detroit. He was married to Millie Monroe.

Mae Johnson was a showgirl, dancer, singer and actress who was well known in the black entertainment world in the 1930s and 1940s. Her career began with nightclub performances  in New York. She had the staring role in the film "Keep Punching." On the west coast she appeared in a few Hollywood films that had black musical numbers, giving a memorable performance in Lena Horne's "Stormy Weather." She recorded some songs with Austin McCoy and co-starred in the hit play "Two in a bed" with Louise Beavers.

Wendell Green was a Los Angeles journalist, and former managing editor of the Los Angeles Sentinel. During World War II, he reached the rank of Lieutenant in the United States Air Force. He was also a champion of the "Don't buy where you can't work" campaign to boycott businesses that refused to hire African Americans.

Actress Mae Johnson in the middle of a a crowd, behind the Musart Theatre (1320 S. Figueroa) the opening night of the play titled Two in a Bed, which she performed in with Louise Beavers. Mitchell Miles is in the foreground with his back to the camera. Sentinel reporter Wendell Green is standing to the left of Mae Johnson. Millie Monroe is standing at the far right, dressed in a light-colored fur coat. The gentleman standing to the right of Mae Johnson is possibly Bob Bennett.

Trumpeter Harry "Sweets" Edison was known best for accompanying vocalist Ethel Waters.
Dorothy Dandridge was a singer and actress, and the first African American to be nominated for an Academy Award for Best Actress for her work in Carmen Jones.
Alice Key was a showgirl who later became a journalist, community activist, and political leader.
Catherine Basie was the wife of Count Basie.

Louis Armstrong was an American trumpeter, composer, singer and occasional actor who was one of the most influential figures in jazz. His career spanned five decades, from the 1920s to the 1960s, and different eras in the history of jazz. (Wikipedia)
William "Count" Basie was an American jazz pianist, organist, bandleader and composer.
Harold Nicholas performed with the dancing duo the Nicholas Brothers.

title_tesim: Successful businessman Dr. C. W. Hill posing with his car, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Charles Wesley Hill (commonly known as C.W. Hill) was born in Hartwell, Georgia in 1900, the son of William and Bessie Hill. He moved to Los Angeles from Arkansas, initially working as a dentist. He quickly became a successful businessman and real estate broker. Hill was active in the NAACP and other civil rights organizations fighting for full integration for African Americans, as well as a well-known member of the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity. Dr. Hill was also a popular figure in African American social circles in Los Angeles, hosting many a party and fundraiser at his Hollywood home (at 5127 Franklin Avenue). According to Walter L. Gordon, Jr., at some point Mr. Hill simply disappeared from the Los Angeles scene, and no one knew what had become of him. Census records indicate that he died in Evansville, Indiana in 1977.
subject_topic_tesim: African American real estate agents--California--Los Angeles
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

He found success with ventures such as the Flamingo Motel and a partnership in Woodlawn Cemetery. He was an avid fisherman, and known to be generous to those who were less fortunate. He died in 1984.

Rich Baker was a business man involved in cafes and other enterprises. He was prominent in political, social and fraternal circles.
Kid North was a well-known expert on racehorses. He is said to have recommended that Charles S. Howard purchase the racehorse Seabiscuit.

Fred Williams, a blind man, was a real estate broker and newspaper publisher. Editor of the Omaha Monitor, he came to Los Angeles in 1923 and organized and published the Pacific Defender in 1925. Later he became a columnist for the Pittsburgh Courier and a contributer to many other newspapers. He wrote a column titled "Through the Darkness I See."

title_tesim: Planning commissioners for the National Association of Negro Musicians, Los Angeles, 1949
summary_tesim: Gwendolyn Harriel was a leading soloist at Independent church.

Albet J. MCneil (b. 1920) is an American choral conductor, ethnomusicologist, author, and founder of the Albert McNeil Jubilee Singers. His career has been dedicated to upholding choral music traditions with the presentation of Negro spirituals and concert music by African American composers. He is Professor Emeritus of Music at the University of California, Davis, where he was director of choral activities and headed the Music Education Program. (Wikipedia)

George Ramsey was a San Diego businessman, and proprietor of the Douglass Hotel, opened in 1924 as a place for both entertainment and lodging for African Americans in San Diego. He also ran a racetrack in Tijuana with his brothers.

Cortez W. Peters, Sr., opened a business and vocational institute to fully prepare African Americans for business and civil service employment. The first school was in Washington, D.C., and others opened in Baltimore and Chicago.

Andrew Sturgeon "Doc" Young was an American sports journalist and author. He was also one of the first African American publicists working in Hollywood. He graduated from Hampton Institute (now Hampton Univeristy) in 1941 with a BA degree in business administration, where he edited the school newspaper.   Throughout his career he received numerous honors from the National Newspaper Publishers Association. In the 1950s, he served in several top editorial positions at Jet Magazine and Ebony Magazine. He also worked in editorial positions at the Los Angeles Sentinel and the Chicago Defender.

Dr. H. Hamilton Williams was a biologist, horticulturalist and landscape architect. Head of the department of ornamental horticulture and the Hampton Institute in 1944, he completed a Ph.D. degree in ornamental horticulture at Cornell University in the same year. He may have been working in Los Angeles by 1949, and was employed as a biologist in turf grass reaearch and education at the Los Angeles County Arboretum up to at least 1970. While at Cornell, he directed the Southside Commmunity Chorus. He was also an organist.
George Garner was a tenor vocalist (and first African American to sing with the Chicago Symphony Orchestry) and conductor.
caption_tesim: Text from newspaper caption: Commissioners confer -- The Central Colony of the LosAngeles Planning Commissioners for the National Association of Negro Musicians, Inc., met at a dinner meeting here last Friday night. Plans for the annual musicians convention, slated for Los Angles August 21-26, were discussed. Shown above seated. left to right, are: Mrs. Evelyn Williams, Cleveland Kelly, Mrs. Lilly T. Hogue, Albert McNeil, state organizer; Mrs. Lillian Peters, Washington, D.C.; Walter Miller, and Mrs. Blanche Porter. Standing, starting at the left are Charles Williams, Horace Pettibone Clark, Jr., George Ramsey, of San Diego; Miss Gwendolyn Herriel, Cortez peters, Washington, D.C.' Rev. George Garner, regional director; Dr. H. Hamilton Williams, Miss Eleanor Richards, secretary local branch; "Doc" Young, Sentinel sports editor, and Hester Orticke. [The person identified as Hester Orticke is a man, and is possibly Lester Lionel Orticke, who lived in Los Angeles at this time.]
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
Photograph appeares in the Los Angeles Sentinel, "Plan National Musicians Convention," 3 Mar.: 1949, A2

title_tesim: Charlotta Bass with Walter L. Gordon, Jr. at the National Baptist Convention of the U.S.A., Los Angeles, 1949
summary_tesim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr. was an African American attorney in Los Angeles.

Charlotta A. Bass was the owner and publisher of Los Angeles' oldest black newspaper, the California Eagle, from 1912 to 1951. The newspaper, which began publishing in 1879, was one of the longest-running African American newspapers in the West. Bass was also a pioneering civil rights activist and erstwhile political candidate. She organized the South Los Angeles Protective League, to deal with the ousting of non-white families from all-white neighborhoods; the League went on to become a leader in organizing against restrictive racial covenants in Los Angeles. She walked in picket lines to integrate the aircraft industry, among others. Bass originaly came to California from Rhode Island as a very young woman, and married J. B. Bass, then the owner of the California Eagle. She retired in 1959, selling the paper to Judge Loren Miller.

title_tesim: Formal event attended by physicians and others at a hall, Los Angeles 1940s
summary_tesim: Tom Griffin was a dentist.
Marjorie Bright McPherson was married to Dr. Henry McPherson. She was a school administrator.
Laura Fowler was the wife of physician Jefferson D. Fowler.
Dr. Frank Robinson was a medical doctor on Central Avenue.
Norma Green was the wife of actor Eddie Green. Mrs. Green was a singer, and she played the piano and violin. The couple had a daughter, Elva Diane.
Dr. Leonard Stovall (1887-1956) was the first African American doctor to be appointed to the staff of Los Angeles County General Hospital.
Sarah Griffin was the wife of dentist Tom Griffin.
Margaret Carter René was married to the composer Otis René.
Beulah Honoré, a former dancer with the Regalettes in Chicago, and also an interior designer and milliner, was married to Gideon Honoré, a member of Duke Ellington's orchestra.
Emily Brown Childress Portwig was well-known for her civic work in Los Angeles and nationally. She attended UCLA and Howard University, and conducted graduate work in bacteriology at USC.
Jefferson D. Fowler was a medical doctor.

Otis René was the brother of Leon René, and with him and Clarence Muse composed the song "When It's Sleepy Time Down South" (Louis Armstrong's theme song). The song was composed at the Baumann-René drugstore on 14th and Central, where Otis was a pharmacist.
Emily Brown Portwig was a Los Angeles pharmacist.
Yolande Stovall was the wife of Dr. Leonard Stovall.

Dr. Clifford Gordon was a physician and surgeon. He was originally from Fargo, North Dakota. He went to USC for pre-medical training and eventually attended Howard University's School of Medicine. He died in 1952 at the age of 51.

summary_tesim: Eddie Burbridge was a journalist, and served as the Sports Editor for the California Eagle before becoming the Theatrical Editor for the Los Angeles Sentinel.
Bessie M. Gant was a columnist from the Pittsburgh Courier.
Leon Hardwick started his career at the newspaper The Call and went on to work as a public relations expert.

Halley (sometimes spelled Hally) Harding was one of the original members of the Savoy Big Five, a precursor to the Harlem Globetrotters. At one point he was a aide to Walter Gordon, Jr. He later became a sportswriter for the Los Angeles Tribune. Along with other African American journalists, he led pickets against all-white baseball teams such as the Los Angeles Angels. When the Rams moved to Los Angeles, they applied to lease the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. Harding made a moving speech at the Coliseum Commission's hearing, arguing that since black tax dollars helped build and maintain the Coliseum, none of its tenants should be allowed to discriminate. He also summarized the contributions of black athletes who had played in the NFL before an unwritten color ban was instituted in 1933, and spoke of the sacrifices of African American soldiers in World War II. Rams General Manager Charles Walsh met with Harding a week after that meeting, at the Last Word, a club on Central Avenue. Harding brought with him influential editors from all three major black weekly papers: the Los Angeles Sentinel, the California Eagle, and the Los Angeles Tribune. Harding's efforts played a key role in the Rams' later decision to sign the black football players Kenny Washington and Woody Strode.
Herman Hill was a journalist and also the first African American to play varsity basketball at USC.
Paul McGee was a Los Angeles Sentinel columnist.

Lawrence F. LaMar was a film critic who was published in several newspapers including the Los Angeles Sentinel, the California Eagle and the Los Angeles Times. He had a column titled Through the Keyhole, and he is quoted in several publications on film.
Louis "Louie" V. Cole began his career as a newspaper reporter for the Sentinel and became a civic leader of South Central Los Angeles. He dubbed himself the "Mayor of Central Avenue."
Leon Washington, Jr. was the founder and publisher of the Los Angeles Sentinel.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Standing at the far left is Halley Harding. Also standing (R to L) are Eddie Burbridge, Lawrence LaMar, Louie Cole, a man identified as one of the Irving Brothers (publishers of the Neighborhood News), and Leon Washington. Seated (R to L) are Paul McGee, Leon Hardwick, and Dorothy Height (Mr. Gordon identified the woman as Dorothy Height, but she might be journalist Bessie Gant). The man seat 4th from the left is Herman Hill. The others in the photograph are unidentified.

Charles A. Williams is documented as a photographer from Oklahoma, practicing in Los Angeles and married to Yoshi Kuwahara in the 1948 edition of Negro Who's Who in California. It also states that he earned the title "Charles of Avalon" while working at the Avalon Studios, the largest Negro owned studio on the Pacific Coast, and that he had photographed for the Pittsburgh Courier, Pacific Coast Edition; the California Eagle Staff and the Los Angeles Sentinel. His obituary in the Sentinel reports his activities in the public relations profession, ultimately becoming the Chairman of Public Relations International.
Dr. T. Edgar Ward was a wealthy man who owned a lot of property in Dallas, TX.

title_tesim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr., with Horace Clark, L'Tanya, and others, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr. was an African American attorney in Los Angeles.
Liz McCullough came to Los Angeles to visit her boyfriend (he had moved from Chicago to L.A. to start a tombstone business).

LaTanya Griffin (or "L'Tanya," or "L'Tanya Martinique") was a society woman, a model and a fashion designer. In her early twenties, she was awarded a contract by the Guild Studios to design gowns for Ida Lapino.  She was contracted to with the Edward D. Wood Production company to design gowns for 20 of its films in 1954, making her the first African American designer to have a contract with a Hollywood film studio. She and Earl Griffin divorced in 1949.

Horace P. Clark, Sr. was originally from Mississsippi and graduated from Alcorn College. He relocated to Chicago for better job opportunities and became a railroad man. He later moved to Los Angeles where he and his first wife, Vera, built the Clark Hotel.

title_tesim: H. Claude Hudson and an unidentified man, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: H. Claude Hudson had a varied career, working as a dentist, lawyer, and businessman. He was President of the first branch of the National Organization for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in Shreveport, Louisiana; after he moved his family to Los Angeles, he was elected President of the Los Angeles branch and served in that capacity for 10 years. In 1931, Dr. Hudson became the first African American graduate of Loyola Law School. He never practiced as a lawyer, however. He also founded Broadway Federal Savings and Loan.

title_tesim: Herb Jeffries performing in Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Herb Jeffries was the first African American actor to star in American western films, such as "Harlem on the Prairie"; he was known as the "Bronze Buckaroo." "Race movies" such as these were among the first to depict African Americans as something other than subservient characters such as servants. He retired from acting in 1939, and went on to sing and tour with Duke Ellington's orchestra.

He opened the Crescent Insurance Agency on Central Avenue (across from the Dunbar Hotel) and later a real estate and construction company at a different location. He was also appointed a Real Estate Commissioner for the State of  California later in his career. He was active in the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity, and participated in anti-segregation efforts in Los Angeles and neighboring areas.
Herman Spurlock, well known on Central Avenue, made a living putting together shows and entertainment for nightclubs and fundraisers.

title_tesim: Walter L. Gordon, Sr., and E. J. Porter in Gordon's real estate office, 1920s
caption_tesim: Handwritten note on photo: "My Dad, in rear."
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
Walter L. Gordon, Sr., is in the very back of the photo, and E. J. Porter is in the front. The woman and other two men in the photo are unidentified.

Walter L. Gordon, Jr.: "My father took a succession of jobs, each job seemed to be better than the previous one. He first started a shoeshine parlor in Santa Monica, 1906. Then he ended up becoming a mounted postal carrier in Pasadena and also a letter carrier in South Pasadena. But he was a very young man when he started and he was in his 20s. To retire from the Post Office in those days you had to go to a certain age. He was still in his 20s and he'd been with them a gang of years. It was regarded as a good job because he was a mounted carrier, had to take care of his horse and all that. But he then went into the real estate business, and he also started peddling those papers I told you about. Along the way, he finally got himself an office at Adams and Central (Adams and Central would be 26th and Central). There's a lovely shack of a building there, and he moved in there. He's sitting in this old office back there, these in the 20s. And that's my father's original first office. That's him in the back.This man's name is E. J. Porter. E. J. Porter became a pretty well fixed guy, and this is their office originally. And that's the beginning."
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection

title_tesim: Laura Bowman, probably with Ossie Davis and Ruby Dee, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Laura Bowman born in Quincy, Illinois in 1881, and developed an interest in the theater when she was a child. She began her acting career in New York, eventually organizing her own group called the Dark Town Entertainers. They toured the United States and Europe. She later helped to organize the Lafayette Players, who included actors such as Cleo Desmond, Clarence Muse, Abby Mitchell, and others. With her husband, Le Roi Antoine, she moved to Los Angeles in 1940. She continued her acting career, both with the Lafayette Players and independently.
Ossie Davis (born Raiford Chatman Davis; 1917–2005) was an American film, television and Broadway actor, director, poet, playwright, author, and civil rights activist.
Ruby Dee (1922–2014) was an American actress, poet, playwright, screenwriter, journalist and civil rights activist.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Mrs. Russell as also an elementary school teacher in Los Angeles for 17 years. She passed away in 1965.

title_tesim: Attorney Vince Monroe Townsend celebrating a successful lawsuit against the Edison Company, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Vince Monroe Townsend, Jr. was an attorney. Admitted to the California Bar in 1943, he was an advocate for abolishing restrictive covenants for minority groups in residential areas inthe late 1940s. From about 1947 to about 1965, he ran for political office at the city and State levels several times. Affiliated with the Republican Party starting in 1956, he returned to the Democratic Party in 1966.  As of 1966, he was a Los Angeles County public defender. In 1987 he was a judge pro tempore in the Inglewood Municipal Court.

Luvenia Harper Nash founded and directed the Luvenia Nash Singers, a gospel group. Nash attended the University of Southern California for four years, and then studied at the National Academy of Music in New York City, receiving a Certificate of Teaching. She taught piano and pipe organ, and was an accompanist for such artists as Clarence Cameron White, Florence Cole Talbert, Bell Salter Tyler, Julian Oliver, Estelle Slater Jackson and Edna Heard.

title_tesim: Leontyne King, Jimmy Sherman, and Ethel (Sissle) Gordon, Los Angeles, 1950s
summary_tesim: Leontyne King was a native of Chicago, who moved to Los Angeles in 1938 with her husband, Celes King Jr. She was a three-term member of the Los Angeles Public Library Commission, where she worked to get more jobs for black librarians. She was also active in a number of business, governmental, religious, and community activities. She and her husband were influential members of African American society from the 1940s to the 1970s. She was also known for her fashion sense. After being named to the Los Angeles Sentinel's list of best-dressed women for many years, she was eventually retired to their "Hall of Fame." She died in 1976.
Jimmy Sherman was the pianist with The Charioteers.

title_tesim: Jack Chase and Lillian Randolph (Chase), Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was born in Knoxville, Tennessee, the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (who went on to become an actress). Lillian's best known role was the maid Birdie Lee Coggins on the Great Gildersleeve, which began as a radio program and moved to television. In the 1940s, she and her husband, boxer Jack Chase, purchased a home in the "Sugar Hill" district (West Adams), but could not move in because of restrictive racial covenants. The US Supreme Court declared such covenants unconstitutional in 1948.
Jack Chase was a West Coast fighter.

title_tesim: Sadie Louise and Dewey D. Davidson at a dance, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Sadie Louise Davidson was a hairstylist who founded the Hair Stylist Guild, and who became the first African American member of the State Board of Cosmetology Advisory Committee. She was also active in many groups, such as the Urban League and NAACP, and helped found the Alpha Wives Auxiliary. She died in 1980, survived by her husband, Dewey D. Davidson.

Dewey Daniel Davidson was a prominent African American businessman in Los Angeles, proprietor of the largest mercantile store west of Chicago. He was also very active in the Alpha Phi Alpha fraternity, serving for some years as the regional director. Some called him by the nickname "T-Town." He was the husband of Sadie Louise Davidson.

title_tesim: Dancers Charles "Cholly" Atkins, Charles "Honi" Coles, Mae Johnson, Alyce Key, and Gary Lambert "Pete" Nugent, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Charles “Cholly” Atkins (1913–2003) was an American dancer and vaudeville performer, who later became noted as the house choreographer for the various artists on the Motown label. (Wikipedia)

Gary Lambert “Pete” Nugent was a preeminent tap dancer in the class act tradition. In the 1930s he was a member of the act of “Pete, Peaches and Duke,” performing in venues such as the Cotton Club. After that he had a solo act “Public Tapper, Number One”.  During World War II he spent his military service touring in Irving Berlin’s show This is the Army. Although Nugent considered himself a tap dancer first and foremost, he preferred “all body motion and no tap”, rather than “all tap and no body motion”. Later, Pete teamed up with Charles “Honi” Coles and opened a dance studio in NYC with called “Dance Craft.” He also performed with Coles in “A History of Tap Dance and its Relationship to Jazz” at the  Newport Jazz Festival, which brought tap back into the public eye.

Mae Johnson was a showgirl, dancer, singer and actress who was well known in the black entertainment world in the 1930s and 1940s. Her career began with nightclub performances in New York. She had the staring role in the film "Keep Punching." On the west coast she appeared in a few Hollywood films that had black musical numbers, giving a memorable performance in Lena Horne's "Stormy Weather." She recorded some songs with Austin McCoy and co-starred in the hit play "Two in a bed" with Louise Beavers.
Alice (Alyce) Key was a Los Angeles dancer who later became a journalist, community activist, and political leader.

Charles "Honi" Coles was an American actor and tap dancer known for his   extraordinary elegance and technical precision. He learned to dance on the streets of Philadelphia and went on to a  career on stage in vaudeville and the concert world, in film, and on television. He served as president of the Negro Actors Guild and was at the forefront of the tap revival starting in the early 1970s. He brought tap to the world of concert dance with his performance in the Joffrey Ballet production of Agnes De Mille's "Conversations on the Dance", in 1978. In 1983 he received the Tony Award, Fred Astaire Award, and Drama Desk Award for best featured actor and dancer in a musical for the Broadway hit, My One and Only.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

At table, left to right: Charles "Cholly" Atkins, Charles "Honi" Coles, Mae Johnson, Alyce Key, and Pete Nugent. Atkins and Coles, preeminent tap dancers, performed together for nineteen years, starting in 1940. Atkins was an expert wing dancer, while Coles's specialty was precision.
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection

title_tesim: Charlotta Bass with two associates, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Jim (James Russell) Goodrich had a rich and varied journalism career. He obtained a journalism degree from the University of Illinois, and earned two masters degrees from UCLA: in journalism and political science. He was an urban affairs reporter for the Press-Telegram (in Long Beach), and an assistant director of information and reseatch for the Joint Center of Political Studies in Washington D.C.. He was with John son Publications for more than 10 years where he was, at various times, Hollywood editor and associate editor of Ebony magazine and West Coast Bureau chief for Jet magazine. He was the public relationist for Sammy Davis Jr. and, afterwards, as an editor at the Los Angeles Sentinel (1966-67).  He went on to teach at Harbor College, UCLA, and UC  Irvine, then became the director of the Mass Media Program at Central Washington University in Washington state.

Charlotta A. Bass was the editor of the California Eagle from 1912 to 1951. The newspaper, which began publishing in 1879, was one of the longest-running African American newspapers in the West. Bass was also a pioneering political activist and erstwhile political candidate.

title_tesim: Garden party at Loren Miller's house, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Miriam Matthews, sister of businesswoman Ella Matthews and lawyer Charles Matthews, was hired in 1927 as the first African American librarian in the Los Angeles Public Library. Matthews was also a civil rights activist, historian, and collector of African American art and primary source materials.

Juanita Ellsworth Miller was the wife of Superior Court Justice Loren Miller. The couple had two sons, Loren Jr. and Edward. A graduate of USC's School of Social Work, Mrs. Miller had worked for the State Relief Administration, the Los Angeles Housing Authority, and later the Los Angeles Bureau of Public Assistance. In 1964, she was appointed southern California's social service administrator for the State Department of Social Welfare.
Dorothy Vena Johnson was a poet and educator. With Juanita Miller, a social worker and wife of civil rights attorney Loren Miller, she founded the League of Allied Arts in 1939.
Hazel Anthony was a widow from Chicago.
Sarah Griffin was the wife of dentist Tom Griffin.

Louise Beavers was a well known African American film and television actress. She was a member of the Sigma Gamma Rho sorority and in 1976 was posthumously inducted into the Black Filmmakers Hall of Fame.
Beulah Honoré, a former dancer with the Regalettes in Chicago, and also an interior designer and milliner, was married to Gideon Honoré, a member of Duke Ellington's orchestra.
DeVaughn was a local activist, known for her knowledge of the Los Angeles social scene.

Clarissa Lovinggood Matthews was a teacher in Los Angeles for 15 years. She was the second African American to be assigned to a junior high school position in the district. She was born in San Antonio, Texas in 1910, and was the daughter of Reuben S. Lovinggood, the founder and first president of Sam Huston College. She met attorney Charles H. Matthews in 1930. They married in 1932 and had one son, Charles H., Jr., in 1948.  Mrs. Matthews died in 1950 at age 39 from surgery complications.
Ethel Sissle (1915-1984) was maried to the band leader Noble Sissle, and later to Walter L. Gordon, Jr.
Emily Brown Childress Portwig was well-known for her civic work in Los Angeles and nationally. She attended UCLA and Howard University, and conducted graduate work in bacteriology at USC.

University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections

Chester L. Washington became the first African American news employee at the Los Angeles Mirror-News in 1955, and in 1962 he went to work for The Los Angeles Sentinel, where he became editor. He eventually purchased a number of newspapers, becoming the owner of Central News-Wave Publications. He died in 1983 at the age of 81.
Gladys Hill, married to Herman Hill, was active in civic and social clubs including the Kansis City Brunch Club.
George Cutler was a commercial photographer. In 1942 he lived at 2710 Griffith Avenue.
Herman Hill was a newspaper reporter and helped to establish the Pittsburg Courier in Los Angeles. He was also the first African American to play varsity basketball at USC.
Lillian ("Lil") Fentress was active in the Zeta Phi Beta sorority, the YWCA, and in the Los Angeles Elks organization. She was married to J. Cullen Fentress.
Maudelle Richardson was the wife of H. Leonard Richardson and an avid golfer.

Leontyne King and her husband, Celestus A. King, were influential members of African American society from the 1940s to the 1970s. She was active in a number of business, governmental, religious, and community activities. She was a three-term member of the Los Angeles Public Library Commission, where she worked to get more jobs for black librarians. After being named to the Los Angeles Sentinel's list of best-dressed women for many years, she was retired to their "Hall of Fame."

Mabel Bernice Scott was an American gospel music and R&B vocalist. She arrived on the West Coast blues scene in 1942. Mabel is most remembered for her 1948 hits "Elevator Boogie" and "Boogie Woogie Santa Claus."

Gertrude Gipson Penland was an  syndicated columnist, editor, nightclub owner, publicist, and civil rights activist. She was a columnist and entertainment editor for the California Eagle and, then, for the Los Angeles Sentinel.

Charles A. Williams is documented as a photographer from Oklahoma, practicing in Los Angeles and married to Yoshi Kuwahara in the 1948 edition of Negro Who's Who in California. It also states that he earned the title "Charles of Avalon" while working at the Avalon Studios, the largest Negro owned studio on the Pacific Coast, and that he had photographed for the Pittsburgh Courier, Pacific Coast Edition; the California Eagle Staff and the Los Angeles Sentinel. His obituary in the Sentinel reports his activities in the public relations profession, ultimately becoming the Chairman of Public Relations International.
Beulah Honoré, a former dancer with the Regalettes in Chicago, and also an interior designer and milliner, was married to Gideon Honoré, a member of Duke Ellington's orchestra.

title_tesim: Sadie Louise & Dewey Davidson, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Dewey Daniel Davidson was a prominent African American businessman in Los Angeles, proprietor of the largest mercantile store west of Chicago. He was also very active in the Alpha Phi Alpha fraternity, serving for some years as the regional director. He was born in Tulsa, Oklahoma, which lead some to call him by the nickname "T-Town." He was the husband of Sadie Louise Davidson.

Sadie Louise Davidson was a hairstylist who founded the Hair Stylist Guild, and who became the first African American member of the State Board of Cosmetology Advisory Committee. She was also active in many groups, such as the Urban League and NAACP, and helped found the Alpha Wives Auxiliary. She died in 1980, survived by her husband, Dewey D. Davidson.

title_tesim: Yvonne Shelton and Katherine Alexander, Los Angeles, 1940s.
summary_tesim: Katherine "Kat" Alexander (née Catherine Porter) started out as a secretary for Walter L. Gordon, Jr. She and sister Yvonne Shelton became bail bonds agents. Together they ran the Shelton Bail Bonds Co., and were the first black women licensed to do so in California. Katherine was also an avid bowler. She passed away in 2000 at age 80.

Yvonne Shelton was a secretary who worked for Walter L. Gordon, Jr. for a number of years. She and sister Katherine Alexandern became bail bonds agents. Together they ran the Shelton Bail Bonds Co., and were the first black women licensed to do so in California.

title_tesim: Andy Razaf and his caretaker, Los Angeles, 1950s
summary_tesim: Andy Razaf (born Andr Andreamenentania Paul Razafinkeriefo) was born in Washington D.C. to the nephew of Queen Ranavalona III of Imerina (now Madagascar) and the daughter of the first African American consul to that country. He was raised in Harlem, and when he was 17 he wrote his first song. He became a celebrated lyricist, collaborating with composers such as Eubie Blake and Fats Waller. (Perhaps his best-known collaboration with Waller was Ain't Misbehavin'.) He was sometimes called Mr. Tin Pan Alley, as a tribute to the nearly 1500 songs he composed during his career. In the early 1950s, he suffered a spinal injury caused by a fall on ice, and was partially paralyzed. Razaf died in Los Angeles, California, in 1972.

title_tesim: Augustus Hawkins and Titus Alexander at the Southern Pacific Station, Los Angeles, 1930s
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: Hugh Macbeth came to California in 1913, and practiced law in Los Angeles until his death in 1956. He founded the Los Angeles Lawyers club, and the Society of Truth and Justice and United Races of the World.
All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
L to R: unidentified man, Augustus Hawkins, Titus Alexander, unidentified woman, Bert McDonald, Hugh MacBeth.

Augustus Freeman Hawkins was elected to the California legislature in 1934, serving until 1963.  From 1963-1991, he represented California in the United States Congress. He was known for his support for civil rights and organized labor. He died in 2007, at the age of 100.

Titus Alexander was a well-known African American civic activist in Los angeles. His father, James M. Alexander, was elected to the Arkansas legislature twice, in 1868 and 1870. A long-time employee in the Bureau of Power and Light, Titus Alexander was also an honorary captain in the Los Angeles Fire Department, who received many civic awards (Los Angeles Fellowship League annual award, the Service Award of the Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce, etc.). He died in Los Angeles in 1952.

Bert McDonald was the first black law school graduate at the University of Southern California (1923) and the first black lawyer in the Los Angeles City Attorney's office, becoming Chief Deputy City Attorney. He was a member of the Alpha Phi Alpha fraternity.
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection

title_tesim: Thomas Dewey and Earl Warren campaigning in Los Angeles, circa 1948
summary_tesim: Sidney Dones was a real estate broker and businessman.  He was the first black graduate of LaSalle Extension School, a school that put out legal course materials for people to study at home.

Norman Houston was one of the first African Americans to earn a varsity letter in football at UC Berkeley. He graduated from UCLA and became an executive with the Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company. He was appointed president of the Los Angeles NAACP in 1964.  He participated in the 1965 Selma, Alabama March. He was Deputy Assistant Secretary for Administration, U. S. Department of Health, Education and Welfare during the Nixon adminisatration 1971-1973.
Earl Warren was an American jurist and politician who served as the 30th Governor of California (1943–1953) and later the 14th Chief Justice of the United States (1953–1969). (Wikipedia)
Jefferson D. Fowler was a physician and surgeon.
B.B. Bratton earned his B.A. from the University of Chicago and worked in Lousiana as a certified public accountant. He passed the bar in Los Angeles in 1924 and practiced law.
William Nickerson, Jr. was the founder of Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company.
Maude Mead Broady was the wife of Judge Earl Broady and lived in the Boyle Heights Neighborhood.

Lawrence F. LaMar was a film critic who was published in several newspapers including the Los Angeles Sentinel, the California Eagle and the Los Angeles Times. He had a column titled Through the Keyhole, and he is quoted in several publications on film.
Louis "Louie" V. Cole began his career as a newspaper reporter for the Sentinel and became a civic leader of South Central Los Angeles. He dubbed himself the "Mayor of Central Avenue."

Thomas Edmund Dewey was an American lawyer, prosecutor, and politician. He served as the 47th Governor of New York from 1943 to 1954. In 1944, he was the Republican Party's nominee for President. (Wikipedia)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

At the center (in striped tie) is Thomas E. Dewey, campaigning to be President of the United States, with Earl Warren (vice presidential candidate) just to the right of him. Various notable African American activists, businesspersons, and journalists are present: L to R: Louis Cole (1st), Dr. H. A. Pearl (3rd, head half visible), Maude Brody (4th), William Nickerson (6th), B. B. Bratton (7th), Thomas Dewey (8th), Sydney Dones (9th), Earl Warren (10th),  Norman O. Houston (13th, white tie and shirt shows), Lawrence LaMar (17th, at the end). The women in the light-colored dress might be Jessie Terry.

title_tesim: Betty Clark, Arthur Wilson with Malvin "Mal" Whitfield, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Mal (Malvin) Whitfield was a 5 time  Olympic track medalist in 1948 and 1952 and member of the Tuskegee Airmen who also spent 47 years in Africa training athletes.
Arthur Wilson was a police officer.

Betty Clark (née Betty Whitfield Wilson), was the wife of Horace Clark. Mrs. Clark, a former fashion designer, had been married to a police officer Arthur Wilson. She divorced Wilson, and later married Horace P. Clark, Sr., proprietor of the Clark Hotel. After his death, she became the full-time manager of the hotel. Betty was also the sister of Mal (Malvin) Whitfield, Olympic track medalist and member of the Tuskegee Airmen who also spent 47 years in Africa training athletes.

Frank Ford LaVigne was the youngest captain in the Los Angeles Fire Department, and during World War II he taught classes in fire prevention at Lockheed. He studied law in his spare time, eventually graduating from Southwest School of Law and becoming an attorney. He married Simietta Johnson in 1936, and they had one daughter, Yvette Marie.

summary_tesim: Thomas R. Yarborough was the chauffeur and handyman to evangelist Aimee Semple McPherson, who gave him property at Lake Elsinore in Riverside County.

repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Thomas Yarborough is the man standing at the far left. Leon Washington is standing at center, wearing a beret. Florita Ware is directly to the right of Washington. Dr. H. H. Towles is seated in back at the far right. Herbert Faires is seated in the front middle. Charlotte Strange is the third woman from the right, wearing a bikini top tied with a bow. Irene Marvin the forth woman from the right, wearing a locket.

Lake Elsinore (in Riverside County), along with Val Verde and a small section of Venice Beach, was one of the few places African Americans could go for recreation during this era. Thomas (Tom) R. Yarborough was the owner of the property on the lake where this photograph was taken.

title_tesim: Party in a personal residence, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Helen Wright was the wife of Crispus Attucks Wright and the sister of John McClain.
Williams, who in 1969 became the first African American judge appointed to the Federal Court west of the Mississippi, was part of a second generation of lawyers in Los Angeles.

Vince Monroe Townsend, Jr. was an attorney. Admitted to the California Bar in 1943, he was an advocate for abolishing restrictive covenants for minority groups in residential areas inthe late 1940s. From about 1947 to about 1965, he ran for political office at the city and State levels several times. Affiliated with the Republican Party starting in 1956, he returned to the Democratic Party in 1966. As of 1966, he was a Los Angeles County public defender. In 1987 he was a judge pro tempore in the Inglewood Municipal Court.
Crispus Attucks Wright was a Beverly Hills civil rights attorney, a businessman, and a benefactor to the Law School at the University of Southern California.

Mr. Finkelberg (first name unknown) was an insurance salesman.

Norman O. Houston was one of the first African Americans to earn a varsity letter in football at UC Berkeley. He graduated from UCLA and became an executive with the Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company. He was appointed president of the Los Angeles NAACP in 1964. He participated in the 1965 Selma, Alabama March. He was Deputy Assistant Secretary for Administration, U. S. Department of Health, Education and Welfare during the Nixon adminisatration 1971-1973.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
Mr. Finkelberg is seated at the far left. Just behind him are Edith and Norman Houston.

title_tesim: Jay Moss, Sydney Dones, and B. B. Bratton with others, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Sidney Dones was a real estate broker and businessman.  He was the first black graduate of LaSalle Extension School, a school that put out legal course materials for people to study at home.
Jay Moss was a politically active Los Angeles chiropractor & artist.
B.B. Bratton earned his B.A. from the University of Chicago and worked in Lousiana as a certified public accountant. He passed the bar in Los Angeles in 1924 and practiced law.

title_tesim: Dancer and actress Mae Johnson in Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Mae Johnson was a showgirl, dancer, singer and actress who was well known in the black entertainment world in the 1930s and 1940s. Her career began with nightclub performances  in New York. She had the staring role in the film "Keep Punching." On the west coast she appeared in a few Hollywood films that had black musical numbers, giving a memorable performance in Lena Horne's "Stormy Weather." She recorded some songs with Austin McCoy and co-starred in the hit play "Two in a bed" with Louise Beavers.

Betty Martina, dancer, teacher and choreographer, was the director of the Betty Martina dance studio, specializing in Spanish-Moroccan dance. She was also  the wife of journalist Lawrence LaMar.

Anne Wiggins Brown was an African American soprano radio and concert singer, perhaps best known for singing the part of Bess in the world premier of Porgy and Bess in Boston. In the late 1940s, Brown moved to Norway, to escape the continuing racial prejudice in the United States.

Dr. C. W. Hill had a varied, but successful, career as a dentist and real estate broker; he was also involved with the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG), an organization dedicated to protecting the rights of minorities in the entertainment world.

title_tesim: International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG) fetes journalists, Los Angeles, Feb. 22, 1946.
summary_tesim: Lena Horne was an American jazz and pop singer, dancer and actress, and civil rights activist.

Morey Stanley Mosk was an Associate Justice of the California Supreme Court for 37 years (1964–2001), and holds the record for the longest-serving justice on that court. Before sitting on the Supreme Court, he served as Attorney General of California and as a trial court judge, among other governmental positions.
Publicist Earl "Red" Griffin was the IFRG's public relations director. He was known for discovering and promoting his eventual wife, LaTanya Griffin.

Dr. C.W. Hill was co-vice president along with Lena Horne of the International Film and Radio Guild (IRFG). Initially working as a dentist, he became a successful businessman and real estate broker. Hill was active in the NAACP,  the IFRG, where he was co-vice president along with Lena Horne, and other civil rights organizations. He was a member of the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity. Hill was also a fund raiser and party host at his Hollywood home. According to Walter L. Gordon, Jr., at some point Mr. Hill simply disappeared from the Los Angeles scene, and no one knew what had become of him. Census records indicate that he died in Evansville, Indiana in 1977.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: Photograph taken at Dr. C. W. Hill's house in Hollywood, at an International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG) program before an audience of journalists.  The purpose of the IFRG was to protect the rights and interests of minorities in the entertainment world.
The man standing 3rd from the left was identified by Mr. Gordon as journalist Ted Le Berthon, but the man's identity is undetermined.
L to R: Stanley Mosk, Earl Griffin, Ted Le Berthon (possibly), Lena Horne (in hat), C.W. Hill (behind Ms. Horne), a man named Silverton (first name unknown), unidentified woman.
All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.
