title_tesim: Sportsmen sitting at a table, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Halley (sometimes spelled Hally) Harding was one of the original members of the Savoy Big Five, a precursor to the Harlem Globetrotters. At one point he was a aide to Walter Gordon, Jr. He later became a sportswriter for the Los Angeles Tribune. Along with other African American journalists, he led pickets against all-white baseball teams such as the Los Angeles Angels. When the Rams moved to Los Angeles, they applied to lease the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. Harding made a moving speech at the Coliseum Commission's hearing, arguing that since black tax dollars helped build and maintain the Coliseum, none of its tenants should be allowed to discriminate. He also summarized the contributions of black athletes who had played in the NFL before an unwritten color ban was instituted in 1933, and spoke of the sacrifices of African American soldiers in World War II. Rams General Manager  Charles Walsh met with Harding a week after that meeting, at the Last Word, a club on Central Avenue. Harding brought with him influential editors from all three major black weekly papers: the Los Angeles Sentinel, the California Eagle, and the Los Angeles Tribune. Harding's efforts played a key role in the Rams' later decision to sign the black football players Kenny Washington and Woody Strode.
Henry Armstrong ("Homicide Hank") was a boxer.
Paul McGee was a Los Angeles Sentinel columnist.
Eddie Burbridge was a journalist, and served as the Sports Editor for the California Eagle before becoming the Theatrical Editor for the Los Angeles Sentinel. He also wrote poetry.
Edward Joseph "Abie" Robinson was the Los Angeles Sentinel sports editor.

Dynamite Jackson (born Ernest Bendy) won the California state heavyweight title, before going on to become the first African American boxing referee in the state. He was also the proprietor of a tavern on Central Avenue that bore his name (it eventually moved to West Adams, near Crenshaw). He died in 1967, at the age of 55.
Rainy Shaw was a liquor store owner.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

At the head of the table is boxer Dynamite Jackson. Seated on the far left is Rainy Shaw. 3rd from the left is Abie Robinson, and 6th from the left is Henry Armstrong. 1st from the right is Paul McGee, 2nd from the right is Eddie Burbridge, and 3rd from the right is Halley Harding. Mr. Gordon stated that the men at the table either played sports or were sports writers. The others at the table are unknown.
member_of_collections_ssim: Walter L. Gordon, Jr./William C. Beverly, Jr. Collection

title_tesim: Judge Stanley Mosk addressing an event, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Allen C. Woodard, III, attended UCLA and Southwestern Law School in Los Angeles. Once he received his real estate broker's license, he became active in fighting racial restrictions on property ownership and occupation. He also served as President of West View Hospital Organization, supporting the efforts of African American physicians seeking to work at Los Angeles hospitals. He was active in many community organizations, such as the NAACP and YMCA, and also an active member of the Omega Psi Phi Fraternity. He died in 1984.

In 1947, Mosk issued a ruling declaring restrictive racial covenants to be unconstitutional. He later was elected California's Attorney General (the first Jewish executive branch officer in California) and was appointed to the state Supreme Court in 1964.

H. Claude Hudson had a varied career, working as a dentist, lawyer, and businessman. He was President of the first branch of the National Organization for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) in Shreveport, Louisiana; after he moved his family to Los Angeles, he was elected President of the Los Angeles branch and served in that capacity for 10 years. In 1931, Hudson became the first African American graduate of Loyola Law School. He never practiced as a lawyer, however. He also founded Broadway Federal Savings and Loan.

Dr. C. W. Hill had a varied, but successful, career as a dentist and real estate broker; he was also involved with the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG), an organization dedicated to protecting the rights of minorities in the entertainment world.

Edwin L. Jefferson was the first African American judge west of the Mississippi River, appointed to the Los Angeles Municipal Court (1941), the Los Angeles County Superior Court (1949), and the 2nd District Court of Appeal (1961).

Honey Murphy is standing, 3rd from the left. To the right of him, in a flowered dress, is Judy Smith.

Honey Murphy's Last Chance cafe on Central Avenue at 93rd Street was raided by vice squad officers on December 7, 1947. The cafe was believed that Mr. Murphy was running a brothel within the cafe. Officers used crowbars, sledgehammers and axes to demolish the fixtures and equipment. After the squad reportedly told Murphy he would not be allowed to operate a cafe at the address, he decided to convert the space into apartments.

Wendell Green was a Los Angeles journalist, and former managing editor of the Los Angeles Sentinel. During World War II, he reached the rank of Lieutenant in the United States Air Force. He was also a champion of the "Don't buy where you can't work" campaign to boycott businesses that refused to hire African Americans.

Eugene Biscailuz was a perennial sheriff year after year in Los Angeles county. He was a very popular person. He rode down the street on a beautiful white horse; he raised horses. He had a son, too, who went into politics.

title_tesim: African American media figures, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Leon Washington, Jr. was the founder and publisher of the Los Angeles Sentinel.
Walter Winchell was a newsman and radio commentator.
Joe "Smoke Rings" Harris was a Los Angeles reporter and newspaper columnist.

Joe Adams was born in Los Angeles in 1924. He overcame racial discrimination to realize his goal of becoming a pioneer radio announcer in the 1940s, on the Santa Monica radio station KOWL. His show soon became the top rated radio show in Los Angeles. In that same decade, he also was known for two television shows, "Adams Alley" on KLAC and "Joe Adams Presents." Adams also appeared in more than 26 motion pictures. In the 1950s, he started working with musician Ray Charles, becoming his manager, producer, and promoter.

title_tesim: Joe Louis seated at restaurant table with Muriel Andrade, Dick Abrams and others, Los Angeles 1940s
summary_tesim: "Dick" Richard Abrams was a member of the original Lafayette Players theatrical troup in Harlem, which also  toured during the 1920s and 30s. He appeared in a few motion pictures as well and was dubbed the "sepia Valentino" in the eastern theatrical circles in the mid 1920s. He moved to Los Angeles in the the 1930s and became active in politics and civic work. AFter WWII, he moved to Oxnard where he founded the J. Finley Elks lodge, was its the first president, and also became the  president of the Negro Political Action Association of California for Ventury County.  In the 1960s he sold his ranch properties in Oxnard and moved back to Los Angeles where he resumed local civic activities on a more limited scale.

Dorothy Dandridge was a singer and actress, and the first African American to be nominated for an Academy Award for Best Actress for her work in Carmen Jones.

Lillian Randolph (later Lillian Randolph Chase) was an African American radio, television and film actress, as well as a singer. She was the younger sister of Amanda Randolph (who went on to become an actress). In the 1940s, she and her husband, boxer Jack Chase, purchased a home in the "Sugar Hill" district (West Adams), but could not move in because of restrictive racial covenants. The US Supreme Court declared such covenants unconstitutional in 1948.

In her early twenties, she was awarded a contract by the Guild Studios to design gowns for Ida Lapino.  She was contracted to with the Edward D. Wood Production company to design gowns for 20 of its films in 1954, making her the first African American designer to have a contract with a Hollywood film studio. She and Earl Griffin divorced in 1949.

Catherine Garcia, Walter L. Gordon Jr.'s secretary for many years, was politically active and worked to motivate other women to be active in civic organizations.

Dr. Omar Stratton, a medical doctor, was a fellow of the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists and served as Chief of Staff of California Hospital. He was also President of the Sickle Cell Anemia Research Foundation, and a member of the Board of Trustees of Chadwick School. He was also an artist and self-taught musician.
George Cannady had a varied career as a firefighter, real estate agent, and lawyer.
Rex Freeman was an attorney.

Dr. Lincoln Shumate was a prominent Los Angeles medical doctor. He worked as a general practitioner, radiologist and child psychiatrist. He was on staff at Cal. State Univ. Fullerton, Metropolitan State Hospital, UCI Medical Center and Fairview State Hospital as well as maintaining a private practice.

Nelson Creswell was the owner of the Cress Bar in 1947, was known as a fastidious dresser and was frequently mentioned in social reporting in the Los Angeles Sentinel. According to Walter Gordon, Jr., he was known as someone who occasionally sold goods door to door, and who never squealed on anyone as to where they got something.
Alice Key was a showgirl who later became a journalist, community activist, and political leader.

title_tesim: Lil Hammond, Louise Beavers, Bea DeVaughn, Leroy Moore and others, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Louise Beavers born in Cincinnati, Ohio, was a well known African American film and television actress.  She had been a member of the Sigma Gamma Rho sorority  and in 1976 was posthumously inducted into the Black Filmmakers Hall of Fame. She died in Los Angeles at age 60 following a heart attack.
Lil Hammond was the ex-wife of Detroit businessman Thomas "Rooster" Hammond. Mrs. Hammond moved to Los Angeles in 1943. She was the proprietor of Lil's Flower Box on west Jefferson.

Leroy Charles Moore was the husband of Louise Beavers. Moore, an interior decorator, was born in Gainsville, Texas. He came to Los Angeles and became well known among theater folk. Moore died in 1963 at age 49.
Bea DeVaughn was active in civic organizations, and was known as a local activist with knowledge of the Los Angeles social scene. In the 1940 census her occupation is listed as dressmaker.

title_tesim: Lena Horne and actress Mae Johnson on the opening night of Two in a Bed at the Musart Theatre, Los Angeles, 1946
summary_tesim: Lena Horne was an American jazz and pop singer, dancer and actress, and civil rights activist.
Jimmy Mundy was an arranger, composer, and jazz saxophonist. He worked with popular musicians such as Bennie Goodman, Count Bassie and Earl Hines.
Lee Gaines was a composer and vocalist, and a founding member of the Delta Rhythm Boys.

Mae Johnson was a showgirl, dancer, singer and actress who was well known in the black entertainment world in the 1930s and 1940s. Her career began with nightclub performances  in New York. She had the staring role in the film "Keep Punching." On the west coast she appeared in a few Hollywood films that had black musical numbers, giving a memorable performance in Lena Horne's "Stormy Weather." She recorded some songs with Austin McCoy and co-starred in the hit play "Two in a bed" with Louise Beavers.
Leonard Reed started his career as a dancer, became an all-around entertainer. Later he kept a booking office in Hollywood.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
description_tesim: All information concerning the content and description of the image was provided by Walter Gordon.

Crowd behind the Musart Theatre (1320 S. Figueroa) on the opening night of Two in a Bed, staring Louise Beavers and Mae Johnson. Lena Horne is standing in the center of the photo in a light-colored fur coat. The man standing behind her (facing forward with mustache) is Lee Gaines. To the right of Ms. Horne is actress Mae Johnson (back to the camera). Behind Mae Johnson is Jimmy Mundy (dresed in top hat).  Leonard Reed is standing in the foreground to the far right. The woman in the foreground on the far left was identified as Spurlock (first name unknown).
This event was covered in the article,    by Wendell Green "'Two in a bed' smash hit; set for long run: spicy boudoir farce hilarious laff hit ," Los Angeles Sentinel, 28 Feb.: 1946: 18.

title_tesim: Halley Harding, athlete and sportswriter, at the piano, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: William Claire "Halley" Harding  (sometimes spelled Hally) was a three-sport star in college and the pros who played for both the Kansas City Monarchs and one of the early Globetrotters basketball teams, tried out for the Chicago Cardinals, and appeared with Fritz Pollard’s New York Brown Bombers.  He played baseball in Japan, the Philippines, and Puerto Rico, and somehow managed to stretch out his varsity college football career over (at least) seven seasons and four different schools (Agate Type: Halley Harding, agatetype.typepad.com). He later became a sportswriter for the Los Angeles Tribune. Along with other African American journalists, he led pickets against all-white baseball teams such as the Los Angeles Angels. When the Rams moved to Los Angeles, they applied to lease the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum. Harding made a moving speech at the Coliseum Commission's hearing, arguing that since black tax dollars helped build and maintain the Coliseum, none of its tenants should be allowed to discriminate. He also summarized the contributions of black athletes who had played in the NFL before an unwritten color ban was instituted in 1933, and spoke of the sacrifices of African American soldiers in World War II. Rams General Manager  Charles Walsh met with Harding a week after that meeting, at the Last Word, a club on Central Avenue. Harding brought with him influential editors from all three major black weekly papers: the Los Angeles Sentinel, the California Eagle, and the Los Angeles Tribune. Harding's efforts played a key role in the Rams' later decision to sign the black football players Kenny Washington and Woody Strode.

title_tesim: Louise Beavers, Hattie McDaniel, George and Netta Garner, and the Southernaires, Los Angeles 1940s
summary_tesim: William Edmondson (bass singer/narrator) was a member of the gospel group the Southernaires.

The Southernaires (1930-1950s) was a gospel group that gained radio popularity. The group's original members were Homer Smith (tenor), Lowell Peters (second tenor), Jay Stone Toney (baritone), William Edmondson (bass singer/narrator), and Clarence Jones (pianist). Homer Smith was later replaced by Ray Yeats, and Clarence Jones was replaced by Spencer Odom. The group briefly had a television show, The Southernaires Quartet, on ABC (1948-1949).

Paul Robeson was an American bass singer and actor who became involved with the Civil Rights Movement. At Rutgers College, he was an outstanding American football player, and then had an international career in singing, with a distinctive, powerful, deep bass voice, as well as acting in theater and movies. He became politically involved in response to the Spanish Civil War, fascism, and social injustices. His advocacy of anti-imperialism, affiliation with communism, and criticism of the United States government caused him to be blacklisted during the McCarthy era. (Wikipedia)

title_tesim: African American business and legal figures, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: William Nickerson Jr. was a prominent Los Angeles-based businessman and founder of Golden State Mutual Life Insurance Company, which at one time was the largest black-owned business west of the Mississippi.

H. A. Howard was a business entrepreneur who founded and was the president of the Broadway Federal Savings and Loan Association. He was very active in civic organizations and was a vice president of the local chapter of the NAACP.
Edgar J. Johnson rose from agent to president of the Golden State Mutual Insurance Company.

(C.) Albert (Al) Maddox founded the Maddox Realty Company, the Maddox Construction Company, was a co-founded  Broadway Federal Savings and Loan Association, and the Chairman of the Board of Directors of the Golden West Real Estate Board. He also participated in numerous civic organizations and was a lifetime member of the NAACP.
George Cannady started out as a firefighter, at a time when African American firefighters were rare. Later he went into the real estate business, and later still he became a lawyer. He died in 1968.

Loren Miller (1903-1967) was an attorney in the 1940s. he later became a California Superior Court Justice in Los Angeles County. Miller was known for his work fighting for equal access to housing and education. He was also the editor of the California Eagle after Charlotta Bass.

Dr. Charles Wesley Hill moved to Los Angeles from Arkansas. Initially working as a dentist, he became a successful businessman and real estate broker. Hill was active in the NAACP, the International Film and Radio Guild (IFRG) where he was co-vice president along with Lena Horne, and other civil rights organizations. He was a member of the Phi Beta Sigma fraternity. Hill was also a fund raiser and party host at his Hollywood home. According to Walter L. Gordon, Jr., at some point Mr. Hill simply disappeared from the Los Angeles scene, and no one knew what had become of him. Census records indicate that he died in Evansville, Indiana in 1977.
Bernard C. Herndon who moved to Los Angeles around 1940. He was a real estate broker and president of the non-profit Stovall Foundation for community health and welfare.

title_tesim: War Perkins, L'Tanya, and Herb Jeffries in a group, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: LaTanya Griffin (or "L'Tanya," or "L'Tanya Martinique") was a society woman, a model and a fashion designer. In her early twenties, she was awarded a contract by the Guild Studios to design gowns for Ida Lapino.  She was contracted to with the Edward D. Wood Production company to design gowns for 20 of its films in 1954, making her the first African American designer to have a contract with a Hollywood film studio. She and Earl Griffin divorced in 1949.

War Perkins was a radio announcer who also worked as a master of ceremonies. He was a partner in Roy Milton's Miltone label, and ran a pressing plant that pressed the Roy Milton/Miltone series of labels. In addition, he acquired the Mosby & Spikes record shop. Perkins appeared in two movies: Slapsie Maxie's (1939) and Sullivan's Travel (1941, as a churchgoer) (1941). After WWII he sold the record shop, went to the Philippines and set up a pressing plant in Manilla.
Herb Jeffries was a movie star, whose character was known as the "Bronze Buckaroo."

title_tesim: Bardu Ali and Tila de los Santos, Los Angeles, 1940s
summary_tesim: Otila (usually referred to as "Tila"), originally from San Antonio, operated a restaurant near the Club Alabam.

Bardu Ali was an American jazz and rhythm-and-blues singer, guitarist, and promoter. From 1921 to 1929 he performed in a precision dance team with his brother Abdeen, called the Ali Brothers. Afterwards, he became master of ceremonies for the bands of Leroy Tibbs, Chick Webb, and Napoleon Zyas. He is credited with persuading Webb to hire Ella Fitzgerald. After 1943 he moved to Los Angeles. He produced shows and was a band leader at the Lincoln Theatre, and, in 1946, produced comedy skits and talent shows at tFollies Burlesk Theatre. From 1948-51 he  performed in Johnny Otis's band and opened the Barrelhouse Club with him, and was Otis' road manager from 1951-63. He is credited with introducing Etta James to Johnny Otis. He played an important role in the early career of Charles Brown and was Redd Foxx's business manager.
